description: Tentacles are almost as long as the body, which is small. Translucent fins end in a point. Distinctively large eye.
Five black body bars tapering towards white abdomen. Bright yellow patch on upper body. Widely forked tail.
Brownish body with five dark body bars. Long upper lip. Rounded tail lobes. Found on shallow rocky inshores.
Females are usually yellow with dark spots on gill cover and base of pectoral fin. Eyes are always bright orange.
Tan body with white and brown spots. Cirrus branches out in a single plane. Blunt papillae on head and snout.
White spots on head, white band behind eye. Many large spines. Cirrus with short branches. Orange patch over nape.
The smallest member of this genus. Short spines and bushy cirrus. Dusky orange eyes. Found in shallows.
Oblique white bar behind eye. Many short spines, often “crewcut.” Single stalk cirrus may have short branches.
Orange-brown girdle almost covers the entire body. Tufts of short bristles extend from sides of each plate.
Brown plates, often encrusted. Girdle with wide dark bands and short bristles. Found close to the waterline.
Deep orange to yellow with small black spots. Horns not present in smallest individuals and develop as they grow.
At night or while resting, the skin darkens and the blue lines become brighter, breaking up their general outline.
A series of darker curved bars (may be faint) start behind pectoral fin, which is edged in a darker blue.
Bright yellow circular body with bright blue margins on dorsal and anal fins. Gold iris circled in bright blue.
Uniformly blue or greenish body with no bars. Blue lines radiate behind eye. Always yellow-orange pectoral fin.
Dark green cup shape on a long slender stalk. Rounded ridges radiate from the center to form bulbous edges.
Round white cup shape on a long white stalk. Rounded ridges with pale valleys radiating from the center.
Red to yellowish-brown carapace. Long tufts of red hair along the tops of claw arms. Pale bands on the claws.
Reddish-brown carapace with two distinctive large dark spots to the rear, each circled in white. Darker claw tips.
Bluish-gray to light brown carapace covered with dark brown lines and blotches. Sharp spines on claw arms.
Tan to gray with tufts of cirri and dark spots in faint lines. Small pectoral fin and fleshy groove at top of gill cover.
Cream to reddish-brown valves. Distinct radial ribs (with fine concentric threads) lead to serrated valve margins.
Only the oral disc appears above sand, with short round tentacles. Retracts into a donut shape during the day.
Flattened oral disc with bulbous warty projections. Tentacles around rim formed into rectangular shapes.
Bright green to brownish body with white spots and lines. Dark lines radiate from eye. Seagrass beds, up to 3m.
Reddish-orange head and front body. Rear body with four reddish-brown bars and spots. May show blue spots.
Reddish-purple body with darker red cross-bars. May be solid red or blue-spotted. Shallow, often under urchins.
Rigid colonies of thin cylindrical branches. Rectangular zooids are clearly visible. Prefers deep reefs and walls.
Transparent dome. Numerous tentacles attach to the bell and also appear to continue onto it in radial lines.
Radial canals extend onto a large belly, half of the bell’s diameter. Sparse thin tentacles, swollen at the base.
Dark gray to black above with white spots and circles. White below. Flattened upturned snout. Small eyes.
Transparent body with short tentacles up to 30 cm / 1 ft. Small individuals may show a greenish bioluminescence.
Slippery rose-red flattened stalks, repeatedly branching to form a low bush. Found attached to deeper substrates.
Rounded shell with a low spire. Tan to black with white blotches. Spiral lines of white dashes. Found in shallows.
Bright orange to brownish body, paler below. Upper jaw extends beyond lower jaw. Found in shallow sand/rubble.
Smooth transparent bell with four long thin trailing tentacles. Sting causes intense pain. Found in shallows.
Long silvery to greenish body with a single tall dorsal fin. Dark oval spot on pointed snout. Found in shallow areas.
Long filaments from behind dorsal and anal fins (shorter with age). Small diffuse dark spot on upper gill cover.
Pale translucent body with white-edged body segments and scattered black spots. Long white lines on head.
Mottled reddish-brown body, sometimes with faint bands. Dark brown and pale spots. Red eye set close to snout.
Transparent body with visible internal organs. One oversized major claw with yellow or purple highlights.
Red body with white spots on abdomen and dark spots above. Green-gold spots on claws and down the back.
Translucent body with eight reddish-brown bands. Large smooth major claw. Often found living in pairs.
Grayish body with irregular bands. Lives in a symbiotic relationship with the Orangespotted Goby (p. 294).
Red body with white spots on abdomen and dark spots above. White patches on claws with no green-gold spots.
Brown body with wide dark bands. Variable patterns, claws may have green speckling and pale orange tips.
Small mouth on elongated snout, distinctively concave below and convex above. Pale dorsal and anal fins.
Yellowish to tan body covered with bright blue lines and black spots. Long first dorsal spine. Distinctively long tail.
Tall barred body. Bright red spots on face and dorsal fin. Red tassels on dorsal spine tips. Dark bar at tail base.
Smaller individuals are usually darker, with fewer fleshy appendages and often hide in lagoons and seagrass.
Loose clusters of thin tan to gold branches that divide near the tips. Found in the shallows among Turtle Grass.
Dense clusters of extremely fine calcified branches. Pale pink to yellowish-green, with distinctively white tips.
Widely branching segments are slightly flattened. Pale pink to white in color. Found growing in dense clumps.
Distinctively flattened segments, repeatedly branching from a common base. Pink to reddish with white tips.
Thin rounded green blades with lighter veins running towards the edges in a distinctively star-shaped pattern.
Ten long pale arms with thin red banding. Red side branches. Can swim about by undulating the arms.
Dark purple spots on claw arms, back and tail. Long thin antennae. Found on Corkscrew Anemones (p. 121).
Rounded body with light brown overlapping plates. Attaches to the head of Blackbar Soldierfish (p. 325).
Distinctive upward curve on sharp rostrum. Pale pink body with small orange spots. Often in tube sponges.
Large diffuse black patch behind pectoral fin. All fins are black. Found in or near the entrances to caves.
Two wide black bars on head, one through eye and another behind gill cover to below pectoral fin. All fins are yellow.
Rounded pale brown to yellow valves with faint growth lines. Pale interior with numerous short white tentacles.
Pale brown carapace, sometimes with a darker longitudinal stripe. Dark-banded claw arms. Found in sand and rubble.
Color varies, often red or black. Transparent spots on tail and fins, mimicking sponges. Large blunt first dorsal fin.
The smallest of the frogfishes. Small brown spots on first dorsal soft ray and at mid-base of dorsal fin. Short lure.
Thin dark lines and spots on head, body and fins. Color varies (may be black). Lure with worm-like appendages.
Flattened yellow to light brown valves with wavy streaks. Widely spaced low radial ribs. May attach to gorgonians.
Occasionally the branches of the Bipinnate Sea Plume will be pale pink, yellow or even dull whitish in color.
Translucent dorsum with dark brown speckles. Mantle fringed with white and yellow, with larger black spots.
Translucent yellowish-brown or green to black. Irregular brown mottling and white patches. An active swimmer.
Tan to greenish-brown body. Numerous dark circles and pale spots. Parapodial wings usually closed over back.
Elongate brownish-red body with white speckles. Dark edges on parapodia and rhinophore and tentacle tips.
Color varies, often brown with pale spots. While moving, a sucker on rear foot anchors it while the body stretches.
Dark purple to black body with some faint mottling. Can sometimes spread its parapodial wings to swim away.
Robust cream to brown body with white blotches. Dark lines along parapodia and rhinophore and tentacle tips.
Red to salmon body with no distinctive markings. Usually a dark line radiating from eye. Found near anemones.
Pale bronze body. Dark bar from behind second dorsal fin down to anal fin. A second dark bar at tail base.
Two small spots behind second dorsal fin, first black then smaller white. Darker tips on second dorsal and anal fins.
Dark spot under second dorsal fin. Stripe from snout to edge of gill cover. White lines on top and bottom of eye.
Bar behind dorsal fin narrows to point above abdomen (compare: A. robinsi). Often a darker bar at tail base.
Narrow dark bar behind second dorsal fin and wide bar towards tail base, each with a narrow white bar behind it.
Pale pink head and body with pearly highlights. Thin dark bar behind second dorsal fin and a wider bar at tail base.
Dark spots under second dorsal fin, at base of tail and on edge of gill cover. White lines on top and bottom of eye.
Reddish-bronze to salmon body with darker speckles. Elongated oval spot at tail base. Fins often pale yellow.
Translucent to reddish-bronze body with thin dark stripes. Dark spot at tail base, sometimes diffuse. Yellowish fins.
Dark bar behind second dorsal fin continues to anal fin. Dark borders on dorsal and anal fins. Wide bar at tail base.
Pink body with orange abdomen. Three dark bars: behind second dorsal fin, near tail and at tail base.
Solid dark blue body and fins. High, curving lateral line. Found on deeper reefs and headlands with current.
Silvery-gray body with five to seven distinct dark bars. Found on rocky shores in coastal areas (not islands).
Rounded bluish-silver body with yellow stripes. Dark spot below lateral line (compare: spot on L. rhomboides).
Only seen by the waste that this worm blows up onto the sand. A small pit nearby is the burrow’s entrance.
Brown body with a silvery sheen near abdomen. Bright orange crescent above iris. Small reddish pectoral fins.
Single greenish-yellow tube extending from the reef. Large incurrent siphon on a bulbous translucent tip.
Rigid pink to grayish stalks with densely packed short pointed branchlets. Gametophyte stage shown above.
Five short triangular arms with blunt tips. Color varies from white to yellow, red or blue. Found among rubble.
Low clusters of fleshy red to iridescent pink leaves. Blade margins are often branched and overlapping.
Fleshy white to light brown body, often with blotches. Short podia on the back and in a skirt around the sole.
Bronze body and dark speckles on fins. Enlarged pelvic fins, often yellowish and with a darker leading edge.
Black to bronze with black speckles. Row of black dots down mid-side. Dark lines behind eye. Black pelvic fins.
Five elongated arms bordered with a series of knobby plates, each with two sharp white spines. Color variable.
Long arms branch repeatedly from a tangled mass near the central disc. Light brown to reddish. Nocturnal.
Dark purple body with radiating red lines. Long spines are clustered in radial bands down the body segments.
Gray body with white spots on head (may form radiating lines). Black stripes on tail (not continuing onto body).
Pale gray dorsum with black blotches. Elevated rows of white tubercles. Gills and rhinophores match body color.
Dark to light brown valves with numerous raised ridges. Sharply pointed end is hidden in reef, sand or sponges.
Elongated body with pale stripes and scattered spots. Eyes set far back from elongated snout. Color is highly variable.
Elongated orange body. Two distinctive white spots on head. White tips on rhinophores and long oral tentacles.
Broad fan-shaped dark green blades with a spongy tex-ture. Lower edge turns upward to attach to the stalk.
Spongy paddle-shaped dark green blade with a distinctively thickened stalk. Prefers clear shallow water.
Pinkish-gray body. White, blue, yellow and red on cerata. White patch on front of head. Rhinophores fused at base.
Dark gray body with bright blue spots and lines. Rarely on reefs, often found taking shelter under floating debris.
Yellowish-gray body with thin dark diagonal stripes and small spots. Usually found close to shelter on the reef.
Pale yellow carapace, legs and claws covered in round orange nodules. Fine yellow bristles on legs and claws.
Numerous transparent tentacles have a spiraling white pattern. Oral disc usually hidden. Found in sandy areas.
Dark head with bulbous snout that extends out in front of the mouth. Golden iris. Found buried in shallow sand.
Mottled brown with three dark saddles. Row of three blotches on lower flank. Dash-like spots along body.
Mottled brown with three dark saddles. Two rows of dark blotches form a checkerboard pattern on lower flank.
Mottled brown with three dark saddles. One row of dark blotches on lower flank. Dark blotch on lower tail base.
Brown dorsum with white spots. Banded pointy cerata. Rhinophores with knobby tubercles. Short oral tentacles.
Pale brown tentacles around a darker brown oral disc. The body is white. Found on deeper sponge species.
Bright yellow tentacles around a darker yellow oral disc. The body is yellow. Found on deeper sponge species.
Tentacles and body are deep purple to maroon. Often found encrusting on Dark Volcano Sponges (p. 51).
Translucent white dorsum with small white papillae and pale blotches. Rolled rhinophores and a wide oral veil.
Smooth uniformly dark orange to reddish body. Broad flattened oral veil. Found in areas of sand and rubble.
Circular crown of radioles. Brown with white bands or solid white. Found in clusters. Visible elongated tubes.
Circular crown of radioles, sometimes banded. Tiny eyespots usually visible. Tubes are sometimes hidden.
Red body with diffuse white stripe from mouth onto side. Large yellow patch from end of dorsal fin onto upper tail.
Blue or purple upper body. Yellow abdomen, fins and tail. Acts as a cleaner species, removing larger parasites.
Transparent body with four wing-like flaps. Internal organs visible at night. Distinct lobes gather in towards the mouth.
Orange to reddish body with small white tubercles and lines. Stocky rhinophores. Feeds on hydroids (p. 136).
Sometimes dark with scattered white spots concentrated around rhinophores. Green color varies as they feed.
Blue rosettes on body and blue spots on head and fins. Dark bars on pectoral fin in larger fish. Notched forehead.
Blue to gold rings and spots. Midbody blotch. Convex (not notched) forehead. Males have long pectoral spines.
Covered in brown to tan rosettes. Dark midbody blotch. Males with orange spots between widely spaced eyes.
Long colonies of rust-colored or yellow to orange-red individuals, each sharing a larger excurrent siphon.
Rose-red tangle of bulbous leaves on long cylindrical stems. Mixed with other algae inside the reef crest.
Translucent reddish-brown body. Banded claws on long claw arms. Nocturnal, often found inside tube sponges.
Brown with white banding and dark spots. Radioles form a distinctive horseshoe shape. Tube is hidden in the reef.
Dark to light brown arms with numerous long thin spines. Wide pale central lines on arms extend onto the disc.
Black ocellus on gill cover. Dark bars extend onto dorsal fin. White stripe on snout. Males develop a bright red rear body.
Small dense clusters of light green feather-shaped leaves with pointed tips. Thin pale green central stalk.
Tall cylindrical stalks of light green, tipped with darker green feather-like branchlets. Grows in dense clumps.
Purple fan of widely spaced stalks, rarely branching and without red tips. Shallows to deeper sheltered reefs.
Brown fan-shaped colony of flattened branching stalks with distinctively red tips. Often under darker ledges.
Chalk-white with red spots on cerata tips. Rhinophores have distinctive swellings and small reddish striations.
Pale tan to whitish shell with brown reticulated markings. White spots on the mantle, often completely retracted.
Irregular flattened body column, covered in large pale bulbous projections (vesicles) when tentacles retracted.

Rows of tiny brown tentacles poking out from crevices in the reef. Pale rounded tips are sometimes striped. Flattened body column with noticeably smaller vesicles than B. antilliensis. Tentacles rarely extended in daytime.
Reddish pedal disc covered with pale warty knobs. Short pointed olive-green tentacles with red and yellow bands.
White to creamy-yellow oval dorsum with distinctive rows of yellow glands. Rhinophores pale to golden brown.
Silvery with bluish scales and brassy cheeks. Blue line under eye. Distinctive orange blotch at corner of mouth.
Bluish cheek with small yellow spots. Curved blue line under eye. Blue spots on pectoral base and top of gill cover.
Yellow spots on snout. Dark spot on upper pectoral base. Distinctively humped forehead. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
Silver with a bluish to lavender sheen. Dark spot on upper pectoral base. No blue or yellow markings on face.
Irregular wavy red lines on carapace form a pattern that dissolves towards the rear. Large claws cover the face.
Lumpy yellowish to brown carapace, shorter than it is wide. Ridge above claws with distinctively sharp teeth.
Pale blotches with yellow speckles, formed by a network of dark lines on top of carapace. Large claws cover the face.
The left claw is larger, with pale yellow tips. Orange antennae and eyestalks. Claws are sometimes pale blue.
Clumps of light brown elongated undulating masses. Attaches to substrate or dead corals and spreads easily.
Pale body with sharply pointed snout. Brown spots on top of head. Brassy iris. Dorsal fin starts close to head.
Found attached to hermit crab shells. Large pedal disc, variable in color from brown to reddish. Pale tentacles.
The largest of the swimming crabs. Olive carapace with blue on all legs and claws. Females have orange claws.
Females with less conspicuous dorsal fin, yellow at front and black behind. Yellow to brown bars on lower cheek.
Pointed conical shell with thin chocolate-brown lines. Similar brown lines on beige mantle. Dark brown tentacles.
Conical red to purplish shell with a pointed spire. Finely beaded spiral ribs. Dark mantle with small white spots.
Robust brown to reddish body. Numerous irregular white spots and small bumps cover the body and arms.
Black body with white blotches. One short round tail and one longer thin tail. Orange lines on head and parapodia.
Orange body, often with white spots. Pairs of hooked orange spines at tail base, larger in males. Black tail.
Dark brown with pale stripes and small orange spots on body. Orange lines on head. Shy, found close to the reef.
Uniformly gray body. Black spot at pectoral base. Small black eyes. Short spike under abdomen, before anal fin.
Dark upper body, paler below. Blue lines on snout and radiating from eye. Black borders on a yellowish tail.
Silvery body with a yellowish tint. All fins and tail are pale yellow. Often yellow marks around mouth and eye.
Silvery-blue with olive upper body. Small dark spot on upper gill cover. Tail lobes with distinctively black tips.
Large blunt head. Silvery body with yellowish anal fin and tail. High lateral line. Black spot on upper gill cover.
Silvery body with large eyes and yellow tail. Line of dark scutes to tail base. Usually a dark patch on lower pectoral.
Silvery to olive body with steep forehead. Spot on upper gill cover. Dark scutes to tail base. Found on deep reefs.
Silvery with bluish upper body. Dark blue to black stripe from snout, over upper body and onto lower tail lobe.
Long thin translucent body, tapering to a point. Lives inside the anal cavity of some sea cucumber species.
Slender body. Rounded dorsal fin starts well behind the pectoral fin. Second dorsal fin has a long trailing edge.
Tall heavy body. Dorsal fin begins above the middle of pectoral fin. Small eye. Upper lobe of tail is the largest.
Dark markings on all fins except for the white anal fin. Distinctive white streak on side and a white abdomen.
Rounded dorsal fin with a diffuse white tip. Pectoral fins are very long and also rounded with white tips. Pelagic.
Distinctively short thick neck and a bulbous head. The tall rounded shell is often encrusted with barnacles.
White valves with dense radial ribs and broken pattern of reddish-brown bands. Found among shallow rubble.
Radial ribs of both valves covered with short knobby projections with white tips. Short translucent tentacles.
Smooth bright red carapace with white spots. Legs have hints of purple and yellow at the joints. Dark claw tips.
Flattened bell with short clustered tentacles, pale brown with blunt white tips. Found upside down in the shallows.
Long flattened green to bluish appendages scattered within the tentacles. Mostly in lagoons and mangroves.
Creamy white shell with two rows of knobs. Short spire. Wavy brown markings. Smaller than the King Helmet.
Robust domed shell with short knobs. Cream to tan. Vertical opening in the front. Lip is a solid cream color.
Three spiral rows of triangular knobs. Bands on aperture lip. Juveniles may have wavy lines similar to C. flammea.
Gray with white on dorsum. Pink cerata with white bands, widest past mid-point. Red stripe on lower rhinophores.
Short bushy tufts of upright branches from low runners. Small irregular branchlets give a rough appearance.
Dark green leaves with wide densely packed branchlets. Grows upwards from thick green runners on the sand.
Small clusters of green stalks, growing upward from the reef, with swollen rounded tips. Forms loose bunches.
Elongated dark green oval blades growing on runners. Short slender stalks. Commonly found on seagrass beds.
Dense clumps of small green balls growing from pointed runners, giving the appearance of clusters of grapes.
Short narrow bluish-green leaves with serrated edges. Grows in dense tangled clumps from green runners.
Tall erect feather-shaped leaves of light green, with fine branchlets. Grows from thin pale runners on the sand.
Tiny leaves in a delicate spiral pattern of light green with pale centers. Grows on long runners to form flat bushes.
Dense bright green clumps of short stalks, with lateral branchlets forming serrated edges. Grows on runners.
Delicate fan-shaped white colonies growing from a common stalk. Found in lagoons and on shallow reefs.
Long sloping forehead and a distinctively darkened lateral line. Found in shallow lagoons and mangroves.
Gray to bluish-black. White spots on scales form stripes. Thin blue lines on cheek in juveniles. Found in Florida.
Dark blue body. Yellow to orange face. Blue ring around eye. Bright blue margins on fins. Often on deeper reefs.
Dark blue body. Yellow to orange head, continuing onto nape and dorsal fin. Blue ring around eye. SE Caribbean.
Red spots on body and all fins. Four black or white spots below dorsal fin. Juveniles have white stripe on snout.
Light brown with dark upper body. White spots above tail base and often below dorsal fin. Black spots on lower lip.
Dense tangles of fine hair-like filaments, repeatedly branching outwards. Uniform reddish-orange in color.
Solid white dorsum with pointed gray tubercles, each circled in black. Black-tipped rhinophores.
Blue body with rows of grayish-blue cerata with orange spots. Long blue oral tentacles and short rhinophores.
Pale white to tan eel-like body, the upper part peppered with dark dots. Dorsal and anal fins not connected to tail.
Pointed shell with numerous small ridges. Usually white to pale yellow or brown, circled with dark brown spots.
Transparent ribbon-like body with a thin central band. Lower edge of cilia may show bluish-green fluorescence.
Tan, becoming yellowish in grass, with midbody stripe and bars. Small black spot on front of dorsal fin. Pink iris.
Rounded silvery body with dark bars. Dark edges on long dorsal and anal fins. Often found in small dense schools.
Large black spot near tail base, circled in white. Thin chevron stripes. Yellow-edged black bar through eye.
White body with yellow fins. Single black bar through eye to dorsal fin. Small black spot on rear dorsal fin edge.
Dark yellow body above and almost white below. Wide dark bar at tail base, running onto dorsal and anal fins.
Silvery white with thin black chevron lines. Four black bars: through eye, behind pectoral, above anal and at tail base.
Large pointed spiral shell with patterns of wavy cream and reddish-brown bands. Mottled brown mantle.
A sedentary limpet-like gastropod found on coral rubble. Calcified plates with radial ridges, often overlapping.
Gray dorsal fin with large black spot. Dark lower lobe on tail. Bluish-black pectoral fin with a pale diagonal bar.
Red to black body with blue spots and faint orange lines. Distinctive white patch on front of head and at midbody.
Dark reddish body with two tapering tails of equal length. Orange and blue stripes. A solid white bar at midbody.
Variable black and white markings, often with pale blue near the head and tail. No orange lines on parapodia.
Distinctively smooth rounded shell. One pair of plates between the eyes (compare: Hawksbill’s two pairs).
Thickened shell with many short irregular protruding knobs. Thickened shell lip. Often covered in red algae.
Elongated dark patch above pectoral fin and another at base of dorsal fin. Spots on body and tail (not on fins).
Yellowish body with honeycomb pattern of dark lines. Large dark patches on back and sides. Fleshy spines held erect.
Large black spots on body and all fins. Wide dark bars on head and body. Fewer spines than the Porcupinefish.
Oval body with greenish-brown plates. Ribbed triangles on plates. Scaled girdle with brown and cream banding.
Each segment has a black line surrounded by white, forming a line down the back. Found in sandy areas.
Shiny mustard-yellow to greenish-yellow sponge, often with dark blotches. Pale radiating veins around openings.
Two long horns on rostrum, connected at base. Oval carapace. Claw arms and the first pair of legs are long.
Bright blue to purplish body. Darker upper body from snout to tail base. Deeply forked tail with black borders.
Blue V on snout. Tail, rear dorsal, anal and pelvic fins yellow. White abdomen and tail base. Florida/N Mexico.
Yellow upper body and bright blue or purple abdomen. Bright blue line runs from snout and over top of eye.
Yellow on tips on dorsal fin and tail lobes. Small white spot behind dorsal fin. Large dark spot at pectoral base.
Dark blue to bluish-gray body. Bright blue spots on head and blue crescent above eye. Found on deeper reefs.
White dorsum with ruffled mantle, outlined in red and bright yellow. Red tips on white gills and rhinophores.
Robust thick-lipped black and white shell. Found near rocky inshores. A commercially harvested species.
Undulating gelatinous mass of soft tubular dull brown stalks covered with fine filaments. Can cover large areas.
Thin-walled encrusting sponge with low star-shaped veins around excurrent openings. Color highly variable.
Reddish-orange encrusting sponge with dark veins and a distinctively transparent outer sheath. Found in shallows.
Translucent to whitish body with white internal structures visible. White lines around siphons. Found in clusters.
Transparent body with purple circles “painted” around the siphons. Often found in dense clusters of individuals.
Opaque, blue to purple body. Siphon rims are paler than the rest of the body. Commonly found in large clusters.
Dark purple head and upper body. Rear abdomen, tail base and anal fin yellowish-orange. Light purple tail.
Pale blue legs, banded with red and yellow at the joints. Large black claws with white spots. Blue eyestalks.
Flattened brown to reddish hemispherical body with a raised pentagonal design on the back. Short spines.
Flat brown disc-shaped body with a dark pentagonal design on the back. Very short spines. Buries in sand.
Flattened carapace with wavy ridges. Brown bands on legs. Commensal with Inflated Sea Biscuits (p. 151).
Smooth fleshy mats of round creeping and overlapping lobes. Dark green and covered in short fine pale hairs.
A bushy growth of rounded dark green branchlets, often covered in very fine hairs. Usually found in the shallows.
Fleshy dark green leaves with rounded edges, often overlapping to form a thick tangle. Two-lobed branchlets.
Pale orange shell with white highlights. Long thick-walled lip and curved aperture. Pale tan mantle and proboscis.
The largest of the anemones. Dozens of long tentacles with blue, red or green bulbous tips. Orange or red pedal disc.
Orange to brown shell with a white spire tip and aperture. Creamy white markings form into bands. Purplish mantle.
Light blue to pale purple body. Dark borders on blue dorsal and anal fins. Small dark pectorals. Nocturnal.
Almost flattened spire with a short pointed spire. Fine shallow grooves cover final body whorl. Red mantle.
Elongated pale orange cone, commonly with central broken band of brown spots on final whorl. Red mantle.
Cone-shaped shell with a short pointed spire. Deep red, often a lighter band towards the middle. Dark red mantle.
Conical spire. Bluish-gray with brown axial streaks. Top of final whorl has low white knobs. Dark red proboscis.
Pale cone with short pointed spire. Pattern of small red to brownish spots or dashes covering the final whorl.
Five tubular arms with rounded tips. Red with irregular dark red mottling. Rows of small pits run down the arms.
Orange body with red markings at the joints. Large claws of different sizes. Shy, found hiding in holes in the sand.
Black markings visible through translucent rounded shell. Long tentacles with black stripes and a long clear foot.
A sea butterfly with distinctive white speckles and broad disc-shaped wings. Can produce a feeding web up to 2 m wide.
Orange body and pale brown to yellow oral disc. Long transparent tentacles tipped with bright orange balls.
Metallic bluish-green with golden abdomen. Males have distinctively pronounced forehead. Found in open ocean.
Translucent to orange body. Long distinctively pointed cerata and rhinophores, with purple rings and pale tips.
Translucent yellow body and orange head. Internal dark bar behind pectoral. Dark blotch near front of dorsal fin.
As in C. tortugae but with small brown flecks, spots on top of head, pair of colon-like spots on pectoral base.
Pale body with yellow to orange markings. Band from eye to jaw. Black blotches on iris. Dark bar at tail base.
As in C. tortugae but with dark X markings along flank, two dark spots at tail base. Prefers shallow silty areas.
Translucent orange with white segments along spinal column. Dark scale edges. Pepper spots on lower body.
Translucent with irregular small brown spots. Distinctive straight line of several tiny black spots along upper iris.
Translucent yellow body. Blue on top of snout and upper edge of eye. Three blue lines from eye, upper one short.
The most common goby. Similar to Glass Goby but no dark scale edges or spots. White dash on pectoral fin.
Pale body with orange to dusky markings. Dark bar at tail base. Black blotches on iris broken into irregular spots.
Pale with brown and white spots. White bridle under eye. Bar (not spots) at tail base. No spot on lower pectoral fin.
As in C. tortugae but with darker blotch on lower pectoral base, dark bar at tail base. Prefers clean, deeper water.
Short broadly rounded rostrum. Tubercles between eyes. Decorates with algae, commonly Halimeda. Reef areas.
Hollow hood-shaped rostrum. Hooked dactyls. Last legs 1.5x longer than carapace. Rubble and seagrass areas.
Short rounded rostrum. Last section (dactyl) of rear legs short, half previous section. Reef areas and gorgonians.
Distinctively white snout. Brown body with regularly spaced white and dark bars. Found on sandy bottoms.
Short upturned snout. Brown body with darker broken stripes. Transparent tail. Usually found in areas of algae.
A rare lavender variation of Shortfin Pipefish, often in shallower water. Darker varieties are found up to 300m.
Pale green to dark brown with bright speckles on dense cerata. Brown line on rhinophores. Found on fine sand.
Black with white patches around eye and outer edge of rhinophores. Black cerata with white spots. White foot.
Bulbous pointed green cerata with orange bands and black spots. Often a small blue spot above close-set black eyes.
Brown to brick-red carapace with darker mottling. Red stripes on walking legs. Claws banded with red to black.
Dark, almost black mantle covers most of the plates’ surface, leaving a thin white line visible down the center.
Elongated thick-walled dark red leaves. Blade edges are rough and crenelated. Found growing in small clusters.
Thin pink stripes from eye to snout. Pink spots behind gill cover continue into a wide pink stripe to tail base.
Dark bar through eye onto cheek. Row of dark midlateral dashes. Males have a dark patch on the lower gill cover.
Rounded valves and deep red mantle folds. Long white, red or pink tentacles. Found hiding in narrow crevices.
Pale translucent body. Straight rostrum, slightly curved at the tip. Stalked eyes. Distinctively elongated claw arms.
Pale brown body. Two large dark spots on both dorsal and anal fins. Spot behind pectoral base and on tail.
Transparent body, often with blue markings. Faint muscles and reddish digestive tracts can usually be seen. Pelagic.
Transparent cerata with irregular white-tipped edges. Long oral tentacles. Feeds on Watercress Alga (p. 23).
Pale body and purple rhinphores. Large translucent cerata have brownish to bright purple tips and thin white lines.
Rounded glossy white valves with thin faint ribs. Found in rubble. Long foot used to move quickly into shelter.
Elongated glossy shell and pale mantle. Thin orange line runs along mantle edge. (Previously C. bahamensis.)
Elongated shell with white triangles on edges of black or mottled mantle. (Previously Cyphoma “black morph.”)
Pale bulbous shell is wider in the middle. Creamy mantle has orange spots circled in black, often covering the shell.
Mantle has thin orange lines bordered in black, arranged in a fingerprint pattern. (Previously Cyphoma gibbosum.)
Rounded shell with longitudinal ridges. Broad broken lines of brown or black and white surround the shell.
Blunt snout and tapering body. Large wing-like pectoral fins extend to tail base, with bright blue spots and lines.
Purple body and claws with red highlights. Blue eyes with distinctive horizontal black bars and tufts of bristles.
Red to purple with short brown bristles. Banded legs. Blue eyes with a distinctive star-shaped pattern.
Brown to purplish stalks with fine filamentous hair-like branchlets. Grows in clusters on shallower reefs.
Tall reddish-brown stalks with short fluffy alternating tufts of branchlets. Found in protected areas on deeper reefs.
Fuzzy cylindrical branches in dense clusters. Found growing on hard substrates in shallow, sheltered areas.
Twenty arms, usually golden. Side branches are tinged with silver and often have black tips. Hides in crevices.
Twenty to forty arms of either yellow, brown or black. Side branches are golden or black with golden tips.
Bluish-green upper body. Often a yellow midbody stripe. No spots on lateral line. Dark spot on upper gill cover.
Elongated silvery body. A line of small black dots along curved part of lateral line. Large scutes near dusky tail.
Slender silvery body with a bluish stripe. Red dorsal and pectoral fins and tail. Pelagic, may school on deep reefs.
Valve edges have a distinctive zigzag pattern. Often attached to gorgonians and encrusted with sponge.
Thick central stalks branch repeatedly to form a loose bush. Often covered in Hydroid Zoanthids (p. 127).
A less common variation with white spines. Individuals may be found with both black and white spines together.
Dirty yellow dorsum with brown patches. Low sheaths on dark rhinophores. Rows of white glands around mantle.
Purplish-brown dorsum with tiny tubercles, rhinophores and gills of same color. Rigid mantle. Feeds on sponges.
Clusters of pink to creamy-red cylindrical branches. Branches are smooth and elongated with sparse hairs.
Elongated bell with visible golden brown branching canals. Long thin tentacles retracted when swimming.
Light green bulbous shapes with small rounded knobs giving them a cobbled texture. Often found in clusters.
Small twin-lobed yellow blades with thin olive-brown bands. Often found with n sheen of green or bluish iridescence.
Bushy structures with flat blades. Distinctively small marginal teeth. Yellow to brownish with light banding.
Twin-lobed pale brown blades growing in small clusters. The tips of each blade are edged in a lighter brown.
Flat interwoven mat of stubby notched blades. Light brown to green, often with a brilliant blue iridescence.
Dense clusters of twin-lobed dark brown blades edged in yellow, with yellow speckles. Can cover large areas.
Clusters of thin flat leaves becoming narrower with each branching. Olive-brown with a bright green iridescence.
Usually orange with white specks, in a tunic with shared excurrent siphons. Found growing in small clusters.
Colony made up of many tiny individuals in a common swollen black tunic. Scattered pale incurrent siphons.
Olive to brown with small black spots. Brown bar through eye. Dark patches above pectoral and at dorsal base.
Light gray with a paler abdomen. Small black spots on body and fins. No dark bars (compare: C. reticulatus).
Thick rough-sculptured shell. Tall major ribs with a wavy pattern. Knobby white mantle with distinct dark bands.
Pale oval shell with distinctive beaded black ribs. Convex in front and concave behind the dark-rimmed aperture.
Pale, with dark stripes on upper body. Dark bar from snout, through eye to tail base. Blue markings on cheek.
Orange-brown with pale blue stripes. Darker bars while resting. Blue markings on snout (compare: D. bivittatum).
Silvery body with large black spot at upper tail base, which may form a saddle. White spine on pelvic fin.
Heavily mottled and spotted body. Flexible dorsal spines. Skin keel along lower body, with a row of dark spots.
Tiny individuals empty into a common deep excurrent siphon. Thin pale siphon rims. Forms globular shapes.
Flattened disc in a variety of colors, usually blotched. Small forked tentacles in lines radiating from the mouth.
Wide flattened oral disc with short rectangular tentacles arranged around the outer edges. Typically solitary.
Flattened white carapace with reddish-brown mottling. Bristled legs. Commensal with Sand Dollars (p. 150).
Smooth uniformly white carapace. Fringe of short bristles on legs. Commensal with Red Heart Urchins (p. 151).
Individual tunicates cluster around the excurrent siphons, giving them a mottled appearance. Color varies greatly.
Small rounded clusters of tunicates gathered around a common excurrent siphon. Typically orange or purple.
Green to brown body, wider towards the rear. Scattered rough papillae. Short rolled rhinophores and tentacles.
Flattened carapace edged in black, with tiny pale spots. Small black and white spots on claws. Red-tipped legs.
Broken pinkish-white line down dorsum. Blue spots on edge of foot. Spots on long oral tentacles. From Florida.
Head with median orange line and two lateral blue lines. Dark jaw area. Cerata and oral tentacles with white tips.
White stripe running down dorsum. Thin white ring on tips of cerata. Found on Upsidedown Jellies (p. 110).
Translucent white dorsum with red speckles. Covered in low rounded tubercles. White gills and rhinophores.
Yellow with black lines between pale tubercles. Honduras only, similar D. nigrolineata has white-ringed tubercles.
Dark body with white spots. White-rimmed sheaths on black rhinophores. Bulbous cerata. Found on hydroids (p. 136).
Dome-shaped bristled carapace with a distinctive dark blotch in the middle. Robust claws are tipped in red.
Smooth flattened bell, pale pink to purplish or orange. Four large tufted lobes and thin tentacles over 20 m long.
Long pointed brown shell with deep axial ridges. Pale mantle with irregular brown spots. Banded tentacles.
Large fish without a host are gray. Small fish with white-edged black stripe and narrow white borders on tail.
Black midbody stripe with pale borders. White borders on tail are wide (compare to narrow on E. naucrates).
Dark body with bright yellow markings of irregular spots and bars, forming a chain-like pattern. Found in shallows.
Five tubular arms with rows of red to orange or pale brown conical spines. Maroon color between the spines.
Short brown spines with a thick base. Black or red body. Found boring holes into the limestone substrate by day.
Dark red body. Short pointed spines all have darker tips and a pale ring around their bases. Prefers shallow reefs.
Translucent orange body and siphons. Grows clustered in sandy reef areas, mangroves and seagrass beds.
Yellow bar on yellow snout. Yellow stripe fades to white, from top of eye to tail. On sponges. Mexico to Honduras.
Yellow snout and stripe over eye to behind pectoral. Blue stripe onto tail. Cleaner species. Absent W Caribbean.
Yellow to white stripe from top of eye to tail. Yellowish snout. Sometimes small spot on snout. On sponges.
Blue stripe through eye, from snout onto tail. Cleaner species. W Caribbean. Similar species: E. oceanops in Florida/Cuba.
Thin white bar on black snout. Thin white or blue stripe from top of eye to tail. On sponges. Belize to Honduras.
Rounded yellow spot on snout. Yellow/bluish/white stripe to dark spot at tail base. On sponges. N/W Caribbean.
Yellow bar on yellowish snout. Yellow stripe from top of eye to end of tail. Cleaner species. SE Caribbean.
Silvery to olive upper body and yellow tail. Two blue stripes, with yellow in between, from eye to tail base.
Long whip-like stalk, tapering slightly towards the tip. Red to orange with white polyps. Found on deeper reefs.
A single stalk branches and re-branches, forming a fan shape. Bright red with white polyps. Found under ledges.
White to greenish body with brilliant red and pale orange spots. Long tapering rhinophores, usually held forward.
Fleshy convoluted parapodia resemble leaves of lettuce. Large pale blotches on sides. Long curled rhinophores.
Pale green to white body. Parapodia lined with red. Red spot on short rhinophores. Distinctive blue eyeshadow.
Light yellow to tan body, often with dark blotches. Short rhinophores. Distinctive white rim along parapodial edge.
Green body with a distinctive cleft on the right side behind the head. Found on Halimeda algae (p. 22).
Light green body with tiny black and white spots. Tips of rhinophores and parapodia lined with orange and black.
Pale green body with white papillae on parapodial edge. Distinctive brown band on lower third of the rhinophores.
Yellowish-green body with white papillae. Three flaps on dark-rimmed parapodia, the middle one distinctively long.
Green with distinctive longitudinal white stripes. Solid black line along parapodial edge. Yellow-tipped rhinophores.
Large head and short rhinophores. White patch on head and on parapodia. Only found on Turtle Grass (p. 11).
White patch on head extends onto short rhinophores. Parapodial edge is paler with a thin solid brown line.
White to light green with papillae on head. Red flecks on parapodial edge. Dark band on short knobby rhinophores.
Green to yellowish body. White lines from round head to rhinophores. Small gap in closed parapodia at midbody.
Mottled orange-brown parapodia, often opened. Olive head and body. Purple acorn-shaped patch on the head.
Olive-green, often with iridescent blue spots. Sometimes has a black tail. Large eyes. Purplish-brown rhinophores.
Reddish to dusky head and body. Pointed snout. Single long banded cirrus. Very long filamentous pelvic fins.
Translucent body. Distinctively long mobile filamentous dorsal spike. Endemic to the Bay Islands of Honduras.
Dark spot and orange rim on tall dorsal fin. Blue spots on head. Divot at upper pupil. Can pale or darken quickly.
Pointed snout. Single long tapering cirrus. Arching dorsal fin with an irregular orange blotch at base of first spines.
Metallic yellow-green upper body with dark stripes. Orange snout. Two dorsal fins are widely separated.
Reddish-brown body. Rows of white spots on upper and lower jaws. Only tips of jaws are touching. Yellow iris.
Uniformly greenish-brown body. May have darker band across nape. Only tips of jaws are touching. Pale iris.
First dorsal fin tall, often white. Last bar narrow, indented at top and not darker. Prominent orange midbody scales.
Long sloping snout. Tall first dorsal fin is usually white. Five oblique body bars equally dark, last is wider at top.
Often has pale blue spots. First dorsal fin usually short. Last body bar is darkest and wider than pale bar in front.
Last two body bars darker. Red to dusky bar at tail base. Breeding males with a bright yellow head. S Caribbean.
Bright red rear body and anal fin. Final body bar black and oval. Base of tail with red bar and then white bar.
Reddish head. Last body bar is black, narrower than the white bar in front of it. Red tail with single thin white bar.
Red with thin white lines and a white bulls-eye on side of carapace. Banded legs and large claws of equal size.
Greenish with dark bars. Pearly-white spots on upper body, dark spots on nape. Prominent dark bars on lips.
Flattened oval shell with low blunt apex. Smooth, with low radial ribs. Olive-green to reddish with white spots.
Dark brown with numerous lighter cone-shaped podia along the back, circled in light brown. The sole is reddish.
Pointed rostrum. Sides of carapace deeply bilobed. Long claw arms. Distinctive triangular white mask on the back.
Reddish-brown spots on body and fins. Three dark saddles below dorsal fin and another above tail base.
Large red spots over a lighter body. Dorsal, pelvic and anal fins and tail all have black margins edged in white.
The largest of the groupers. Gray to greenish body with pale blotches. Small dark spots cover head and pectoral.
Reddish-brown body with scattered white spots. White blotches behind eye and sometimes on snout. Dark tail.
Five dark brown to red body bars. Distinctive large black saddle on upper tail base. Gold-tipped dorsal spines.
Elongated dorsal and pelvic fins. Often yellowish color. Pelvic fin usually held downward. Small line down snout.
Dark bars: across eye, from nape to pelvic fin and from tip of dorsal fin to tail base. Rear body and tail spotted.
Translucent body with dark green lines. Inflated white-tipped cerata. Found on Green Bubble Weed (p. 19).
Only adult males will have a large tail that extends well beyond the shell. Claws on flippers are long and curved.
Spiny rostrum and a distinctive constriction in the area behind eyes. Translucent with pink and purple highlights.
Rounded clumps of single-celled tubular branchlets, narrow at the base and branching out near the top.
Mottled red and orange encrusting alga. Forms a rough fan shape with darker lines radiating from the center.
Bulbous flexible body with yellow stripes on the sides. Ring of long pale feeding tentacles around the mouth.
Translucent gray with brown and white spots. Few long and very bulbous cerata. Bands on smooth rhinophores.
Light red to brownish body. Thick tapering spines with light brown banding. Short white secondary spines.
Silvery body with a bluish tint above. Oblique dusky bars along upper back. Dusky outer edge on first dorsal fin.
Silvery body, slightly darker above. Distinctive black tips on front spines of first dorsal fin, with white area below.
Tiny individuals under a common globular black tunic. Protruding siphon edges may be speckled with white.

Iridescent reddish-brown body. Centipede-like legs on each body segment. Found in areas of shallow rubble. Bobbit worms make a den of strong fibers, appearing like spider’s silk. They are largely nocturnal hunters as most of their predators are fish that are active during the day.
Thin white tentacles radiating from a common hole in the reef. Body is hidden. Found close to sandy areas.
Elongated body with short oral lobes. Cilia appear as lines of flashing red spots running parallel to the gut.
Narrow flattened body with dense tufts of white bristles. Orange, red or greenish, often darker towards midbody.
Rounded silvery body with narrow tail base. Oblique black stripes on upper back. Dark spots behind pectoral.
Brown oral disc with a whitish central mouth. Tentacles of different lengths. Common in lagoons and mangroves.
Pointed shell with faint spiral lines and long aperture lip. Pink mantle with white speckles. Short pointed tentacles.
Rounded cerata with white markings. Rhinophores with distinctive bulges. Feeds on the eggs of other sea slugs.
Yellow dorsum with purple spots. Gold line on ruffled mantle edge. Brown-lined gills. Pale purple rhinophores.
White dorsum with large black blotches. Broad yellow band around mantle edge. Purple gills and rhinophores.
Brownish body with small purple spots on dorsum and sides. White gills lined in brown. White rhinophores.
Blue dorsum with dark blue rhinophores and gills. Mantle edged in yellow with a thin red margin. Prefers shallows.
Dark blue to black with yellow lines on dorsum. Yellow margin on white mantle edge, interrupted at midbody.
Large red areas on dorsum extend down to become lines on a white mantle edge. Pale blue spots circled in red.
Red areas on dorsum do not extend to mantle edge. Patterns for this species are more varied than F. binza.
Elongated body with rows of pale blue spots. Tail with long trailing filament. Often found in shallow seagrass.
The color of the digestive tracts in the cerata varies from red to bright orange or yellow, sometimes as a thin band.
Widely spaced cerata with orange bands, connected by thick white lines. White-tipped rhinophores and tentacles.
Thick round blades, often fused and overlapping. Red to maroon, with brownish edges. Found in dark reef areas.
Three large dark ocellated spots: on lower dorsal fin, on midbody and on tail. Elongated filaments on lure.
Clusters of calcified cream to rose-red tubes, branching to form rounded domes. Gametophyte stage shown above.
Heavy body with short wide snout. Series of dark body bars and markings. Upper lobe of tail is the largest.
Tangled mass of soft thick branching stalks. White to light brown. Slimy appearance. Can cover large areas.
Parapodia overlap, covering a small shell. Single siphon above head. Swims when disturbed, or buries into sand.
The largest of the mojarras. Silvery body with diffuse broken dark bars. Distinctive yellow pelvic and anal fins.
Glossy oval shell with a blunt spire. Cream to tan base color with a series of dark brown wavy longitudinal lines.
Tapering white body with four dark or red saddles across back. Tall flexible first dorsal fin. Often buried in sand.
Gray with black and white speckles. Dorsal fins set close to long tail. Fleshy barbels over top lip. Small white eye.
Black body with nine narrow bright blue bars. Red to pale brown head and red Iris. Found in clear shallow waters.
Bulbous head with no obvious rostrum. Dorsal fin has a distinctively long base. Body thickens towards tail base.
Blunt snout. Dark bar runs from top of head, through eye and onto cheek. Commonly a gold spot above pectoral.
Dark sides with broad white stripes. Thin white stripes on top of back. Found on West Indian Sea Eggs (p. 151).
Yellow body with brown bands. Gold on legs and tail. Found on or near sea cucumbers, urchins and sea stars.
Brownish body with bright gold spots circled in black. Long transparent legs and antennae. Transparent tail.
Flattened body tapering to the rear, with scattered brown spots and irregular blotches. Small thin white tentacles.
Pale brown body with small olive spots. Wide flattened head. Found among coral rubble in very shallow water.
Dark bars extend slightly onto dorsal fin. White patch on front of dorsal fin. Orangish lower head with white spots.
Greatly elongated first dorsal fin, extended into filaments in males. Large white-edged black ocellus on gill cover.
Blunt snout. Head is often reddish. Bars do not extend onto the dorsal fin membranes. Second bar wide at top.
Dark bars extend onto fins. Front of dorsal fin is short. Prominent ocellus on gill cover with orange posterior rim.
Overall pattern black and white. Front of dorsal fin tall, edged with white band. Often a dark circle on gill cover.
Reddish head, usually with irregular patches of orange. Dark bars extend onto very spotted dorsal and anal fins.
Light brown body and tapering tail. Elongated dark blotch below dorsal fin, outlined in green. Found in silty areas.
Bluish-gray body with rows of yellow spots. Wavy yellow lines across cheek and onto gill cover. Bright yellow iris.
Head and front of body purple, golden-yellow towards tail. Dark spot on front of dorsal fin. Dark stripe through eye.
Purple body with dark patch from snout to dorsal. Often thin gold lines on head. Usually deeper than G. loreto.
Tapering brown body. Black vertical fins fused with tail, paler at the base to the front. Found deep inside caves.
Pale brown with distinctive loose folds of skin. Around fifteen dark narrow vertical crossbars and more on tail.
Thin tightly spaced branchlets alternate along a dark brown central stalk. The shape resembles a tall feather.
The largest of the morays. Uniformly dark green body with no distinctive markings. Often under ledges in the daytime.
Dark brown head and body covered in small yellow spots that become denser towards a yellow tail. Golden iris.
Irregular black spots over a pale background. Patterns vary greatly. Dorsal fin often edged in black. White iris.
Colors variable from brown to purplish. Dorsal fin black with thin white margin. Yellow iris. Dark inside mouth.
Uniformly silver body with no distinctive markings. Long sloping forehead. Small white iris. Often in sandy areas.
Silvery with bronze-yellow stripe from snout to tail base, another along upper side. Large black spot at tail base.
Coppery stripes down a pale silvery body. Dark pelvic, rear dorsal and anal fins. Black tail. Bright blue eyes.
Silvery-gray with broad yellow stripes across head, continuing to tail base. All fins and tail are bright yellow.
Most juvenile grunts have dark stripes and tail base spot. Only the French Grunt has diagonal body stripes as well.
Thin black stripes on upper body. Wide stripe from eye. Nape and upper back yellow. Yellow saddle at tail base.
Wide black stripe runs from dorsal onto black tail, which is edged in white. Faint yellow body stripes. Pale fins.
Silvery-gray. Dark spots on scales form thin diagonal stripes. Tail and fins are dark, except for a pale pectoral.
Bright blue stripes on a yellowish body. Black tail and rear dorsal fin. Pectoral, pelvic and anal fins are yellow.
Five dark yellow to bronze stripes along upper body. Pale abdomen. Blunt snout. Schools on deeper reefs.
Metallic bluish-green upper body with dark stripes. Yellow snout. Two dorsal fins are connected near base.
Colors vary from pale to greenish. Two spots, black and pink, at upper corner of gill cover. Dark stripe to tail base.
Two ocellated blue spots, one at rear base of dorsal fin, one at tail base. Yellow patch from snout tip onto dorsal.
Yellow body. Brilliant blue stripe, outlined in black, runs from behind eye to tail base. All fins are translucent.
Large dark midbody blotch. Yellow face with red lines on cheek and around eye. Colors and patterns may vary.
Blue stripes on snout and above eye. Reddish dorsal fin. Dark blotch at tail base, orange stripe onto center of tail.
Blocky markings with stripes over head, or rarely a solid yellow-green. Bright yellow eye. Found in seagrass beds.
Orange-brown body, often white blotches on upper back from nape to tail base. Blue lines from eye and on cheek.
Long chains of rectangular dark green segments, joined down the middle by a single stalk. Found in dense bushes.
Bushy clumps of thin fan-shaped green leaves with distinctly pointed three-lobed blades. Found in crevices.
Chains of smaller rectangular green segments, joined down the middle by a single stalk. Forms dense bushes.
Chains of thick calcified dark green segments with three distinct lobes. Forms low bushes from a common base.
Low chains of calcified bead-like spheres with no visible stalk in between. May attach to sand or rocky substrates.
Tall bunches of calcified and segmented stalks growing upwards from a common base. Found in the shallows.
Dense clumps of rounded three-lobed leaves. Dark to yellowish-green. Found in darker areas of the reef.
Calcified dark green blades growing in a single plane or forming into a low bush. Thicker than similar species.
Oval to kidney-shaped calcified green blades growing in a single plane. Prefers shallow waters near mangroves.
Thin rounded fan-shaped blades growing outward in a single plane from a common stalk. Green to yellowish.
Short dark green leaves with an elongated oval shape, growing in pairs. Grows on a short stalk on runners.
Tall bright green oval leaves. Three to four close-set pairs growing on a long upright stalk. Grows on runners.
Long thin central stalks. Tiny alternating branchlets with tiny polyps. Usually found growing in small clusters.
Flattened gelatinous stalks, seldom branching, form a ball shape. Pink to reddish-brown. Prefers deeper reefs.
Translucent yellow to brown with dark spotting. Notched cephalic shield. Parapodia cover only the front of shell.
Brownish-gray parapodia barely cover a speckled shell. Buries in the sand and mud, mostly in shallow areas.
Colonies of small white bristled worms. Only found on the inside walls of Touch-Me-Not Sponges (p. 67).
Iridescent bluish-green with faint dark stripes and four orange stripes. Distinctive orange spot above gill cover.
Creamy light brown scales with a distinctively rough texture of pointed tubercles. Thick tapering cirri on sides.
Light brown rounded oval scales with a rough texture from low tubercles. Pale cirri and dark feeding tentacles.
Rough-edged light brown scales with rough tubercles and fine papillae. Scale edges darker down the middle.
Rounded tan to pinkish scales covered with fine hairs. Tufts of light brown bristles and pale cirri from under the scales.
Golden brown scales with paler edges. Distinctively long pointed teeth on the lateral edges of each scale.
Translucent with black lateral stripe from snout, through eye to tail. Mimics small juveniles of Bluehead Wrasse.
Highly variable in apex height and form. Concentric threads cross the ribs, giving a latticelike appearance.
Dark golden-yellow shell with nine axial ribs. Chocolate- brown between the ribs. Red mantle with white speckles.
Bluish upper body. Elongated lower jaw with a bright orange or red tip. Upper lobe of tail is orange or yellow.
Translucent body with white and brown spots. Elongated transparent cerata with pale cross-like markings at tips.
Even very small juveniles of just a few millimeters are armed with widely spaced tufts of stinging bristles.
Tan body with numerous thin dark brown longitudinal stripes. Row of white spots runs down medial line.
15 white bands across a dark body, with orange spots where they meet tufts of bristles from each segment.
Parapodia with tufts of bristles line the sides. Pale blue iridescent stripes. Some describe as variation of H. picta.
Dark head with pale spots and white abdomen. Found in burrows, forming large colonies on deeper sand patches.
Distinctive red stripes from antennae and through eyes, joining on tail. Found on Corkscrew Anemones (p. 121).
Deep red to pale pink body, sometimes with silvery bars. Large red eyes. Dark specks on tail and dorsal/anal fins.
Pale orange to dark red dorsum and thickened mantle edges (only when disturbed). Red rhinophores and gills.
Light yellow to black blotched body with thin irregular stripes. Thin white lines radiate from eye, over gill cover.
Often in dark colors and found in shallow seagrass beds, they are released from the brood pouch fully formed.
Transparent body with two white bands across the back to the sides. Small white eyes. Found on gorgonians.
Bright orange zooids form a calcified layer. Often found on mangrove roots and dock pilings as well as on reefs.
Iridescent dark blue body above and silvery below. Dark pectoral fin with broad clear margin and short clear bar.
Numerous fleshy tabs and small white spots. Found camouflaged in floats of Sargassum Seaweed (p. 24).
Rounded teardrop-shaped body with spots and bands. Tapering tail. Fast-moving predators, hence their name.
Yellow snout, front abdomen and pelvic fin. Middle body bar is straight (compare to all curved bars in H. ciliaris).
Blue with yellow scale edges. Spot on nape, circled in pale blue. Blue on lips and edge of gill cover. Bright yellow tail.
Golden-yellow body with large diffuse black blotch, often containing a bright blue circle. Blue ring around eye.
Pink with reddish stripes. First dorsal fin yellow. Second dorsal, pelvic and anal fins white to pale pink.
Bright white triangular blotches behind tips of dorsal spines. Elongated rear dorsal fin. Long tail base, long upper tail lobe.
Light rusty-brown with gray or reddish spots and short papillae. Leaf-shaped oral tentacles. May bury in sand.
Creased tapering body in brown, yellow or red. Often mottled. Numerous sharp podia along back and sides.
Slender mottled brown to reddish body. Tapers more towards the mouth. Rows of cone-shaped papillae.
Light brown to black with numerous creases along the body. The sole is red to white with tiny brown parapodia.
Rounded golden-brown body with irregular darker brown spots and blotches. Small pointed papillae on the back.
Tan body tapering at the rear, mottled with rows of brown blotches. Podia of various sizes, tipped with tiny papillae.
The largest sea cucumber. Pale brown body with dark bands. Short tentacles around mouth. Mostly nocturnal.
Smooth and rounded translucent body. One large major claw. Globular black eyes. Found hiding in tube sponges.
Rounded translucent bodies with reticulated pattern of branching white internal organs. Short dark tentacles.
Oblong shell with dark mottling and small orange spots. Orange spots and white blotches on mantle and proboscis.
A thin layer of white to purplish encrusting alga. Grows on hard surfaces like shells. Also on Sea Pearls (p. 18).
Light brown to yellow tentacles on a darker oral disc. Found encrusting the Feather Bush Hydroid (p. 136).
Gray to olive-brown body with protruding eyes. Snout and tips of fins are pointed. Often found buried in sand.
Reddish-brown head with orange spots. Dark bars are thinner and paler near abdomen. Found in Florida/Cuba.
Large head and tapering body, both with small orange spots. Branching cirrus over eye. Found in shallows.
Orange spots on head and front of body. Rear of body with distinctive black and white bars. Found in shallows.
Upper head, back and front dorsal fin bluish-brown. Rest of body and fins yellow. Blue lines and spots on head.
Reddish-brown body, paler near abdomen. Pale blue on mouth and front of anal fin. Blue pelvic fins. Orange iris.
Bright blue head, body and front of dorsal and anal fins. Clear tail with black borders. Found in Florida and Cuba.
Head, body and fins golden-yellow. Black spot on snout, outlined in blue. Blue spots/lines around eye and cheek.
Dark brown body with yellow head and fins. Black spot on snout, outlined in blue. Blue spots around eye and cheek.
Pale blue body with dark blue bars. Blue bar under eye and across gill cover. Pale dorsal and anal fins and tail.
Black, gray or dark brown body and fins. May display tinges of blue. Pectoral fins are usually solid black.
Dark bar below eye. Dark spot or saddle at tail base. Blue pelvic and dark pectoral fins. Dark borders on tail.
Dark bar under eye and from nape to pectoral. Wide dark bar below front of dorsal fin. Small blue spots on snout.
Brown body with yellowish abdomen. Black spots on snout, pectoral base and usually at tail base. Yellow iris.
Uniformly pale yellow body. Large black spot or saddle at tail base. Black spot on snout (sometimes absent).
Greenish-brown head and body, covered in blue-outlined bright red to orange spots. Large dark patch behind eye.
Background color from white to pink or yellowish. Brown blotches become smaller near margin. Pointed tentacles.
Translucent brown body. Bristled claws have reddish lines in a reticulated pattern. Found in sandy areas.
Long banded tentacles surrounding a white oral disc. Central tuft of short pale to lavender tentacles. Nocturnal.
Stiff tubular body with a rough surface. Mouth usually bent inwards. Grows out of holes in the reef. Nocturnal.
Plates colors are highly variable, including green, purple, orange or pale pink. Girdle often with faint dark bands
Faint chevron grooves along the plates. Color varies widely from orange to purple, pink or gray.
Thin flattened valves and a dark brown interior mantle. Found on shallower reefs, often encrusted with sponges.
Thin light brown valves with radiating white lines and visible concentric rings. May grow in dense clusters.
Brown or tan to yellow body is creased and covered in small knobby podia, usually in sharply contrasting colors.
Smooth rounded cream to tan body with brown blotches and small spots (darker around short pointed papillae).
Bluish body. Slender conical snout. Dorsal fin starts just behind the pectoral. Both tail lobes are the same size.
Small tangled clumps of very fine calcified branching stalks with pale tips. Shades of pink with white joints.
Clusters of long pink stalks growing upwards from a common base. Short pink branchlets tipped in white.
Translucent gray with white speckles. Sharp tubercles on long inflated cerata. Caruncle between tall rhinophores.
Elongated semi-transparent body with a single silver stripe. Found in shallow waters, singly or small schools.
Thick oval to triangular, semi-glossy white valves. Thin gray growth rings and dark hinge usually visible.
Flattened pale dorsum with purplish-red dots. Gills and rhinophores are darker, with small brownish speckles.
White dorsum covered in tiny papillae. Rhinophores and gills are pale. Tiny white glands around mantle edges.
Carapace with orange patches outlined in red. Red legs and claw arms banded with white. Prefers deeper reefs.
Longitudinal red lines and white blotches. Branching red appendages on oral veil. Red on gills and rhinophores.
Brownish-gray body with translucent fins. Pale head with nostrils above upper lip. Tiny translucent pectoral fins.
Dark body with large eye and prominent white spots along lateral line. Large oblong glowing light organ under eye.
Grayish-brown to blackish body. Scale rows form dark lines. Large triangular anal fin. Tall rounded dorsal fin.
Silvery oval body. Anal fin and tail not aligned. Similar species: Darkfin Chub (K. bigibbus) has darker fins.
Silvery-blue with thin brassy to gold body stripes. Rear edge of anal fin aligned with top leading edge of tail.
Indistinct dark bars not extending over dorsal fin. Ocellus as in L. cricota (compare to white ring in L. nuchipinnis).
Prominent dark bars extend to edge of dorsal fin. Ocellus on gill cover is indistinct, with a smudgy orange outline.
Usually dark brown. May be black, tan or greenish. Ocellus on gill cover is fully rounded with complete thin white ring.
Dark forehead and stripe on upper body. Three long thin dorsal spines. Yellow pectoral fins. Black bar on tail.

From about the size of a pea. Yellowish body with black or brown spots. The tail is tucked in against the body. Yellow or white body with dense black spots. Often dark bands running between eyes and across upper back.
Tan to greenish-gray body, sometimes with light blue spots. Distinctive rounded hump on back. Elongated tail base.
Dark body with small pale spots. Dark patches at base of dorsal and pectoral fins. Pale honeycomb pattern on side.
Smooth polished-looking valves. Cream to yellowish with orange or brown marks that may be zigzag, as above.
Numerous thin radial ribs, often covered with commensal sponge, as seen above. Short red and white tentacles.
Bluish-silver with blue and yellow stripes. Dark spot on top of lateral line. Yellow anal fin with bright blue edge.
Fleshy mantle covers the entire shell. May be confused for a sea slug; distinctive single raised siphon at front.
Thick-bodied creamy white shell with rounded knobs. Spiral cords with white and brown spots on final whorl.
Highly variable in color and markings, solid brown or with white bands or blue spots, Sharply upturned rostrum.
Short bush of cylindrical pale pink branches. Alternating branchlets with distinctively red tips and fine filaments.
Numerous long transparent tentacles from a hidden oral disc. Small densely packed knobs of stinging cells.
Prominent hump on the back. Often two ocellated spots on lower body. Found in the shallows and on seagrass.

Head with orange line and two leaf-like marks. Red rings on bulbous cerata. Bluish band at base of oral tentacles. Translucent to orange with dark cerata. White on head, white stripe down body. On hydroid P. disticha (p. 137).
Small brownish knobs on thin branching finger-like tentacles. Often with pale brown to white highlights.
Disc-shaped body with a pentagonal design on the back. Six elongated holes around the edges. Very short spines.
Bluish-white shell edged in orange. Short orange stalk. Found in clusters attached to floating debris and boats.
Smooth plates, often red with white spots. Rusty girdle, characteristically without overlapping scales. Uncommon.
Two distinctively long horns on rostrum, almost touching. Two slightly curved white spines at rear of carapace.
The largest comb jelly. Long warty protrusions on body and large oral lobes, which may have brown markings.
Dark brown shell with knobbed ribs and a single thin white spiral cord. Deep red mantle with white speckles.
Pale rounded valves with fine ribs with small spines. Interior mantle and short tentacles are red to lavender.
Pale oblong valves with tiny spines on radiating ribs. Long pink to reddish tentacles, often with faint white banding.
Greenish-brown body with white patches on dorsum. Row of pink circles and yellow spots. Thick warty cerata.
Four to seven rounded arms with blunt tips. All arms can be of different lengths. Color varies from orange to tan.
Thimble-shaped bell is transparent on top and brown beneath. Few short tentacles. Often in large blooms.
Orange with purple stripes. Black ocellus on rear dorsal fin. Ocellated spots on tail not joined (compare: L. rubre).
Uniformly red body. Black bar on tail, edged in white. Black spot on tip of rear dorsal fin. Yellow patch on snout.
Striped body. Dark spots on both rear dorsal and anal fins. Spots on tail are joined. All spots edged in white.
Yellow body with three short blue lines, from snout and behind eye. Found on deeper reefs, often under ledges.
Round flattened shell with curved triangular spines arranged spirally from the center. Prefers shallows.
Robust conical shell with knobs around the lower edge. White mantle with speckles. Often covered in red algae.
Pink to bright red plates of varying thickness, formed into rounded floral shapes. Only found on deeper reefs.
Reddish shell with white blotches. Distinctively flared tips grow both above the eyes and at rear of aperture lip.
Orange to brown shell is pink inside. The proboscis and eyestalks are mottled. Small eyes with black tentacles.
Thick brown shell with two distinctively large spikes on the top. Yellow inside shell. Front aperture lip curves up.
Variable thin fan-shaped blades. Shades of green to light brown. Often overlapping and covering large areas.
Pale brown body with dark blotches and lines. Brown bar through eye. Mimics floating sargassum or dead leaves.
Reddish body with numerous long banded tentacles. Sharp bristles along the body used to move inside a sandy tube.
Short arms and tentacles on a compact body, often with tiny black spots. Rounded fins connect at rear of body.
Translucent gray to light brown with white and brown spots. Rhinophores distinctively tall with conical tips.
Males with raised fleshy crest along midline of head. Dark stripes behind eye. Shallow coasts and mangroves.
White shell with scattered brown blotches. Evenly sized concentric ribs run to a slightly scalloped shell edge.
Flattened oval shell with alternating large and small ribs and finely toothed margin. Mantle color highly variable.
Relatively large aperture. Distinctive concentric threads over the ribs, giving the appearance of a file’s surface.
Reddish body and yellow tail. Long pointed snout. Yellow stripe from snout to nape. Three black spots at rear dorsal fin.
Smooth tan shell with scattered dark spots. Light brown mantle. Black tentacles. Also found in solid gray to black.
Distinctive small black spot on upper flank. Faint blue line under eye. Reddish tail and pectoral fin. Red iris.
Olive to yellowish body, pale near abdomen. May display body bars. Yellow fins and tail. Prominent canine teeth.
Pale gray to light brown body. Yellow patch on upper tail base and a yellow tail. Black spot at base of pectoral fin.
The largest of the snappers. Heavy body with large head and thickened lips. May display contrasting body bars.
Uniformly gray to olive body with a sloping forehead. May display darker bar through eye. Rounded anal fin.
Olive to bronze with a distinctive white triangle under eye. Often blue spots on gill cover. May show faint body bars.
Pale body with a single dark midbody spot. Stripe from snout to top of gill cover. Pelvic and anal fins are pale.
Silvery body with several yellow stripes. Often a single dark spot under soft dorsal fin (may lighten or disappear).
Rounded bell, often with marbled black markings. Bulbous oral arms. Shorter clusters of white tentacles.
Twin crowns of thin black spikes. Found in short tubes constructed of sand. Retracts quickly when approached.
Brick red to mottled dark brown with distinctively dark, almost black, eyes. Darker red banding on claw arms.
The largest of the mantis shrimps. Golden antennal plates with dark speckles. Found in rounded burrows in the sand.
Pale reddish-orange with dark red stripes. Distinctive curved red line where abdomen slopes down to tail.
Yellow legs and body with two broad red stripes down the back. Long white antennae. Often found in pairs.
Translucent body with thin red stripes. Two distinctively curved bands near abdomen. Commonly found in pairs.
Pale body with numerous white podia. Short red, purple or white spines in sparse rows, following the plate lines.
Pale body with red lines between each segment. Green spines. Short podia ending in clear or small purple balls.
Wide dark brown bars. Thin white lines in interspaces. Red dorsal and anal fins. Dark spot at pectoral base.
Reddish to orange body with no bars. Rounded orange spots below eye. Males have long first dorsal spines.
Bright red to orange with prominent barred pattern, bars become stripes to rear. Dark zigzag bars over abdomen.
Uniform reddish to orange with faint bars alternating with thin rows of white spots. Internal white band at midbody.
Prominent wide orange bars. Blue-gray interspaces with midline thin white lines. Two orange bars below eye.
Two non-parallel horns on rostrum. Postero-lateral spine has two lobes (compare to pointed on M. trispinosum).
Postero-lateral spine broad, continuous with body. Short horns on rostrum (compare to long on M. trispinosum).
Two adjacent horns on rostrum. Postero-lateral spine is sharply pointed. Usually found encrusted with sponges.
Oval shell has white spots with brown centers (banded in juveniles). Mantle color variable, gray to brown or black.
Rigid stalk with only a few branches. Distinctively bright white polyps run in pairs down both stalk and branches.
Thick-walled pale shell with a creamy color inside. Body whorl with large rounded spikes. On sand and seagrass.
Distinctively long narrow arms. Gray to light brown with widely spaced white spots. Usually found in sandy areas.
Only the male has very large claws, often longer than width of carapace. Brick-red above, white on abdomen.
Yellow stripe from eye, broadening towards tail. Large black spot on tail base. Hovers close to a small burrow.
Three prominent dark anterior bars, often fusing to form a smudgy H shape. Lower head usually strongly barred.
Yellow head with pointed snout. Ocellated blue spot on first dorsal fin. Usually found near Giant Anemones (p. 116).
Heavily speckled greenish-gray body. Distinctive dark-rimmed, green to bluish square spot on upper rear body.
Faint bars and heavy dark/light speckling. Distinctive ocellated blue spot on upper rear body, onto dorsal fin.
Color varies, with dark upper body and pale lower body, with indistinct blotches and heavy dark/light speckling.
Saddled Blennies are highly variable in color, from tan to yellowish to red-spotted. The saddles may be indistinct.
White to pale brown scales with thin black margins. Dark crescents on inner half of each overlapping scale.
Flattened blades of purplish or red to pink alga with a distinctive network of holes and frills on the outer edges.
Silvery body with distinctively large scales and a large upturned mouth. Dark grayish fins. Mostly nocturnal.
Long sharply pointed white shell with reddish highlights. Dark eyespots. Parasitic on sea cucumbers (p. 152).
Dark blue to black with pale blue-white lines along base of dorsal and anal fins. Blue lines radiate above eye.
Orange-brown shell with small pointed spire. Final whorl with row of short spikes. Dark stripe on brown tentacles.
Rounded dome-shaped body, flattened on the bottom. Short brown spines. Buries into sand during the day.
Palp worms bury into sand, extending two tentacles to feed. This species is recognized by its colored bands.
Translucent body with pale red markings and long thin antennae. Buries quickly into sand when disturbed.
A fine network of thin tubular threads that interconnect. Pale to dark green. Can cover large areas of the reef.
Short snout. Pale irregular body bars and sometimes a thin brown central line running the length of the body.
Two rows of irregular brown blotches. White lines under eye. White bar behind pectoral. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
Bluish-gray body. Iridescent blue stripes below eye. Builds burrows in silty bottoms. Florida/W Caribbean.
White shell with reddish-brown wavy lines. Pale mantle with large white spots and greenish-yellow margins.
Translucent body with small spines and large claws. Found in shallow coral debris and dead conch shells.
Bright red body curving upwards near the tail. Large red claws with pale tips. Found in areas of sand and rubble.
Dark blue with iridescent blue spots over entire body and head. Bright blue borders on fins. Translucent tail.
Carapace and legs are hairy. Smooth red-banded claws. Most commonly found under larger anemones (p. 116).
Deep grooves and knobs on a mottled green to reddish carapace. Three blunt spines to the rear. Banded legs.
Carapace with long grooved indentations. Two sharp spines found just inside the claw arms. Smooth claws.
Knobby green carapace with green legs and claws. Red spot inside joints of claw arms. Claws tipped with white.
Wide red carapace and bristled legs. Numerous conical white spines on claw arms. Blunt spines around eyes.
Glossy elongated brown shell with a long spire. Round to oblong pale spots. Brown proboscis with white blotches.
Olive-brown on top. White below, becoming gray at rear edges. Mouth on underside (compare: M. aff. birostris).
