Large dewlap on abdomen. Pronounced hump behind dorsal spine. Color varies, usually a white patch on side.
Sharply elongated snout. White reticulated body pattern. Can change colors quickly to match surrounding corals.
Thin-walled encrusting sponge in shades of pink to red. Large excurrent openings with dark red radiating veins.
Thick-walled shell with irregular knobby surface and rusty brown patches and dark lines. Heavily spotted mantle.
Yellowish with brown spiral ribs and a large body whorl. Spotted mantle. Juveniles covered in hairy protrusions.
Orange to yellow shell with a sharply pointed spire. Elongated aperture with a distinctively toothed lip.
Small dense bristles cover entire carapace and walking legs (last pair are longest for holding sponge in place).
If the animal has not been feeding, the cerata’s digestive tracts are empty and it appears pale or even transparent.
Distinctive yellow stripe from eye to tail base. Deeply forked yellow tail. Two thin fleshy barbels under chin.
Pink to dark red and may have a pale stripe. Claw arms sometimes banded with yellow. Distinctively long claws.
Pink to dark red, often with a broad white stripe. White- banded legs and claw arms. Distinctively short claws.
Grayish-brown to black body with darker rectangular patches. Thin pale margins on pectoral fin and tail.
Sharply defined dark brown upper body and white lower body. White stripe from chin to bright yellow dorsal fin.
Light brown body with rectangular brown spots (paw prints) arranged in stripes. Dusky tail with pale margin.
Bright yellow body with an irregular dark brown stripe running from lower lip, through eye to near tail base.
Pale lines outline irregular dark blotches on upper body, wavy lines on flank. Broad yellow margin on pectoral fin.
Large white spots on brown to greenish body. Small yellow spots on head, circled in white. Protruding nostrils.
Tan body with bright yellow spots inside diffuse black blotches, smaller towards snout. Protruding nostrils.
Distinctive black bar at edge of gill cover to pectoral base. Thin white margins on all fins except the pectoral.
Uniformly pale tan, speckled with small brown spots on upper head and body. Found in shallows and seagrass.
Jet-black body with two small lobes on the tail, one slightly longer than the other. May show thin white lines.
Green to translucent body with white patches and orange spots. White stripes on separated clusters of cerata.
Yellow shell with pale blotches. Reddish mantle with numerous highly branched projections. Found on rubble.
Rounded white shell with a blunt spire and well spaced ribs. Mantle and proboscis are pale with white blotches.
Rounded shell with thin brown bands. White mantle with reddish-brown markings: lines in front and spots at the rear.
Small translucent dome with eight thin tentacles, half the width of the dome, with round yellow bulbs at each base.
Light brown to reddish body with white blotches or faint lines. Distinct lobes on head. Blue spots on parapodia.
Yellowish to brown or gray with pale abdomen. Blunt rounded snout. Second dorsal fin almost as large as first.
Forty long black arms. Numerous short black side branches, each tipped in white. Short curved legs.
Brown to greenish body with pale spots. Greenish-blue edges on abdominal plates. Colorful club-shaped claws.
Short cylindrical white stalks with green tips. Grows in dense clusters, fused at the base. Tips appear fuzzy.
Green calcified stalks with darker tips. Grows in clusters, but not fused. Usually only found in very shallow water.
Red body with thin white stripes. Dorsal fin red with white tips. Large black blotch across first three dorsal spines.
Golden stripes on reddish body. White-tipped spines on first dorsal fin. Distinctively long white anal fin. Red tail.
Brownish-red with wide silvery stripes and thinner dark stripes. White leading edge on pelvic and anal fins.
Smooth-walled lumpy reddish-brown to tan encrusting sponge. Scattered excurrent openings with paler rims.
Transparent smooth rounded body, generally taking on color of the host gorgonian. White eyes on short stalks.
Pairs of dark blotches down side, often joining to form an H shape. Dark bands on tail. Elongated first dorsal spine.
Elongated brown shell with large white patches and smaller white spots. Longer aperture lip than M. ocellata.
Globular yellowish to translucent gray body covered with short unbranched papillae. Internal organs are visible.
Alternating brown and white bands on a circular crown of radioles. The tube below is usually hidden in the reef.
Circular yellow crown of dense radioles with numerous thin dark bands. The tube is usually hidden in the reef.
Translucent white body with visible internal organs on medial line. Black eyespots, set well back from margin.
Translucent white with a network of thin white lines. Similar markings on oral tentacles, head and long cerata.
Dense clusters of fleshy-textured deep purple branches. Branchlets slightly flattened and tapered at the tips.
Uniformly dark under arms (compare: O. insularis). Color and patterns variable. Eye is dark when startled (above).
Green to bluish body mottled with brown; may change color rapidly. Distinctive dark brown ring around the eye.
Two distinctive dark blue ocellated spots near the eyes. These will disappear when the octopus is camouflaged.
Distinctive red reticulated markings under arms. When startled the eye is white with a dark bar through the iris.
Short flattened body with widely spaced lobes and visible musculature. Comb rows run perpendicular to the gut.
Transparent to milky-white translucent body. Most often with four brown to black spots on the rounded oral lobes.
Bright yellow line from snout, through eye to a widely forked yellow tail. Large pale yellow spots on upper body.
Pale body with green and red highlights. Club-shaped claws. Green and red antennal scales with a black spot.
Gray to coppery body with dark spot at pectoral base. Large reddish eye. Closely related to the drums (p. 348).
Pale brown with yellowish-orange patches behind eye. Dark spots on pale pectorals. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
Dark brown to grayish body. Prominent rostrum (horn) above reddish lips. Pectoral fins may have darker edges.
Raised head with short rostrum. Conical spines on back. Small mouth with reddish lips. Color is highly variable.
Dense mat of long filamentous coppery-brown threads. Attaches to the substrate or hard corals and gorgonians.
Tangled mass of filamentous threads, pinkish inside and brown outside. Often grows on and around coral heads.
Elongated silvery body with a low concave forehead. Clear to dark yellow tail. Most often in sandy shallows.
Rounded bell, wider than it is tall. Purple radial canals. Short primary tentacles, long thin secondary tentacles.
Clear bell with four radial canals. Thick primary tentacles surrounded by numerous thinner marginal tentacles.
Smooth creamy-white shell with a pointed spire. Dark brown wavy interconnecting lines surround the shell.
Five tubular arms, all of the same length. Each arm with a distinctively upturned red tip. Color is highly variable.
Smooth tan to purplish dome, may have yellow spots. Spiny dark brown arms, spines are banded with yellow.
Blunt snout. Red lower lip and pectoral fin. Wide mouth with comb-like teeth. Perches on shallow coral heads.
Smooth central disc in solid gray or black. Arms have prominent blunt spines, becoming shorter near arm tips.
Green to gray with distinctively banded arms. Central disc solid green, speckled or with bold irregular patterns.
The central disc has ten spots, each outlined in black, arranged around the rim. Arms are usually banded.
Long arms with very short spines. The smooth central disc and the arms are covered in tiny brownish spots.
Smooth reddish-brown disc. Arms are always in a contrasting color with darker bands and tiny spines.
Long thin arms have short spines and are banded with red and white. Color and patterns are highly variable.
The central discs of some individuals can be solid color, mottled or spotted pattern of red, green, white or brown.
Brown to light green central disc with thin dark lines in a reticulated pattern. Long arms with thin dark bands.
Long thin arms with light banding and numerous short spines. Arms extend only at night, disc remains hidden.
The central disc has five distinctive lobes bulging out between the arms. Long spines on arms. Color variable.
Silvery blue with thin dark stripes. Spot above gill cover. Black tips on tail. Thin trailing filament behind dorsal fin.
Yellow head and pale bluish body. Dark marks on head. Bar through eye. Rounded tail. Found in sandy burrows.
Gray to light blue body with diffuse pale yellow lines on dorsal and anal fins. Yellow tail. Blue markings on head.
Yellowish-brown body with blue stripes. Blue on top lip and iris. Distinctive thin blue line from cheek to gill cover.
Head sparsely covered in small pores. Finger-like nasal cirrus. 2nd spot on dorsal fin far back, not touching base.
Head densely covered in small pores. Dark branched nasal cirrus. 2nd spot on dorsal fin dark, touching base.
Bars and small pores on jaw. Finger-like nasal cirrus. Adults: no dorsal fin spots, juveniles: one spot near front.
Bulbous translucent bell and numerous thin tentacles. Distinctive club-shaped organs where tentacles attach.
Black with distinctive white patches: near eyes, under chin and on abdomen towards tail. Rarely seen, pelagic.
Five short triangular arms. Thick reddish-orange body with a network of raised red bumps in radiating lines.
Pale to reddish elongated body. Triangular fins towards a distinctively long pointed rear. Pelagic, rarely on reefs.
Distinctive hump on the back of the shell. Attach to Black Corals (p. 90). Often encrusted with a layer of sponge.
Green or yellowish body with conical white papillae and sometimes blue spots. Often on Caulerpa algae (p. 13).
Greenish body with blue spots on closed parapodia and on rhinophores. White papillae on parapodia and foot.
White with large light brown to orange spots down side. Large black spot at lower-rear edge of first dorsal fin.
Flattened reddish carapace with a distinctively wide front between the eyes. Banded legs. Found in the shallows.
Flattened green carapace with dark brown lines. Yellow spots on claw arms. Pink claws. Found in the shallows.
Rounded spiraling thick-walled blades found growing in dense clumps. Blades concentrically banded in white.
The carapace, legs and claws are a uniform bright red. Indentations on claws. Green eyes on yellow eyestalks.
The claws are of equal size with red bands and scattered small white spots. Gold antennae and eyestalks. Blue eyes.
Claws of equal size. Carapace, legs and eyestalks are covered in tiny white spots. Short light brown bristles.
Flattened claws of equal size. Small blue eyes on red eyestalks (compare to gold eyestalks on P. erythrops).
Elongated pale eyestalks tapering to small reddish eyes. Banded antennae. Bands of short brown stripes on legs.
Transparent body with thin dark brown lines and bands. Found in shallows on mangrove roots and under docks.
Squat reddish-orange body covered with fine hairs. Found deep in crevices, caves and on deeper reefs.
Gray to orange with white spots. Bulbous cerata with white nipples. Pale oral tentacles with thin orange bands.
Clumps of short cylindrical reddish-brown stalks. May have iridescent bluish-green bands near tips (above).
Short tentacles arranged around a raised circular disc. Grows in loose clusters within a common fleshy base.
Large flattened disc, often mottled with patches of white. Lines radiate from a pale central mouth. Short tentacles.
Reddish carapace with large white spots on abdomen. Black and white horns above eyes. Long spiny antennae.
Dark red to brown. Upper legs and body are covered with small white spots. Brown stripes on the lower legs.
Highly variable in color: tan, reddish-brown to yellow. Pale blue lines below eye. Branching cirrus over eye.
Reddish-orange. Long fleshy appendage on chin. Three blue ocelli on rear dorsal, outlined in yellow and then red.
Short first dorsal fin. Rear dorsal fin: one small ocellus or none. Eye and nape cirri are flattened bifurcated tabs.
Tall first dorsal fin. White forehead. Two small ocelli on rear dorsal fin. Single stalk eye cirrus. No nape cirrus.
Very tall first dorsal fin. Two ocelli on rear dorsal fin, one on rear anal fin. Branched single stalk cirrus over eye.
Tall first dorsal fin. Orange-ringed bluish ocellus on rear dorsal fin. Branched single stalk cirrus over eye and nape.
Dome-shaped carapace with nodules in contrasting colors. Short bristles on claw arms. Found in rubble.
Color varies from red to purple. Yellowish abdomen. Red spot at pectoral base. Usually on outer edges of reefs.
Pale brown body with three brown stripes. Black spot on first dorsal fin, bordered in white. Schools over drop-offs.
Body has numerous black and white longitudinal stripes. The shortened dorsal fin loses its white leading edge.
Slender orange body banded in red. Long thin legs. Often found on Giant Barrel Sponges (p. 64) at night.
Small red to brown body with few papillae and broad banding on arms. Juveniles live in empty clam shells.
Translucent salmon to bronze with paler abdomen. Indistinct dark stripe from snout, through eye to gill cover.
Rough tan to yellow body with dark blotches. Tufts of small bristles on flat antennal plates. Small black eyes.
Small domed circular to oval colonies, depressed in the center. Pale to deep bluish purple zooids in single rows.
Yellow body with tufts of thick dense cerata with white speckles and tips. Yellow rhinophores and oral tentacles.
Rounded bell with dark yellow or purple spots. Color ranges from translucent pink to yellow. Four oral arms.
Triangular red carapace, covered in red sponge and often small zoanthids. Purplish claw arms with darker spots.
Silver to gray hatchet-shaped body with no distinctive markings. Found schooling inside caves and caverns.
Translucent body with bands of tiny brown speckles across the back. A commercially harvested species.
Tufts of bristle-like light green branchlets growing from a common stalk. White skeletal material on the surface.
A rounded ball shape of tightly packed filaments growing from a stout green stalk. Often a darker shade of green.
Tightly packed filaments end in a flat top, forming an inverted cone. Elongated stalk. Found in sandy areas.
Branchlets alternate from a single stalk. White polyps on tips of each branchlet and stalk. Often found in clusters.
Flattened carapace, circled by a distinctive thin green line. Yellow bands and red spots on legs. Red eyes.
Light brown body with darker blotches, concentrated down the medial line. Widely spaced pseudotentacles.
Translucent tan to pink body with white speckles. Large claw arms. Lives under encrusting tunicates (p. 135).
Transparent body with a thin red stripe. Widely spaced eyes on long eyestalks. Found on black corals (p. 90).
Pale body with gold eyes and stripe on the back. Sides are transparent. Found only on Golden Crinoids (p. 147).
Translucent reddish body with distinctively white-tipped claws. Found only inside Touch-Me-Not Sponges (p. 67).
Dark brown body with white eyes, eyestalks and tail. Found only on Black and White Crinoids (p. 147).
Gold to reddish body with white highlights. Banded legs. Wide band on tail. Found on Giant Basket Stars (p. 146).
Transparent body with brown, white and reddish spots. Patches on the back. Found on Sun Anemones (p. 117).
Pink to tan body with serrated head and purplish-red spots. Transparent sides. Found on Swimming Crinoids (p. 147).
Transparent body with large spots down the back and sides. Purple spots on head. Bands on claws and legs.
Smooth flattened brown valves with dark gray to black mantle. Found in clumps on mangrove roots and docks.
Smooth brown valves with bright green margins. An invasive species, recently introduced to the Caribbean.
Greenish-yellow with faint striations visible inside body. Found in loose clusters under ledges and overhangs.
Highly variable in color and markings. All have a small opening in the parapodia, at middle-rear of the dorsum.
Green to reddish-brown body with a pattern of pale conical knobs, each outlined in white. Prefers shallows.
The largest of the hermit crabs. Rough scaly texture on oversized claws. Long banded antennae. Pale eyestalks.
Greenish-brown with thin dark transverse bands. Bristled legs and claws. Often found in lagoons and oyster beds.
Distinctive white cross on frontal region, between eyes. Outer edge of claws bristled. Brownish bands on legs.
Deep red encrusting alga. Smooth surface, often shiny. Outer edges may not touch substrate, often overlapping.
Rounded pink to red encrusting alga with faint concentric and radial lines. Margins slightly raised from the substrate.
Flattened rounded encrusting alga. Orange-brown to rusty base color. Concentric pattern of dark red lines.
Rows of dark crescent-shaped spots. Oblique dark bar down from eye. Dark bar on entire width of tail base.
Pink with single dark spots on scales. Spots on head are often larger. Dark bar under eye. Bar at tail base.
Silver to bronze body with a yellowish snout. Multiple speckles of different sizes are found on each scale.
Solid black body and siphons with no distinctive markings. May be paler when growing under ledges.
Distinctive blue line runs along dorsum, divides on the head and continues onto long white-tipped oral tentacles.
Color varies from gray to dark brown. Distinctive pointed lobe at midbody. Two equal-sized rounded lobes on tail.
Legs and claws are orange to cream with thin dark red stripes. The right claw is much larger. Bluish eyes.
Maroon body with a large right claw with white spots. Bright orange legs. Blue eyes on banded eyestalks.
Bright orange body, legs and claws. The right claw is much larger. Legs and claw arms have thin white bands.
Light brown body with numerous spots and a darker medial line. Distinctive thin black line around the margin.
Translucent green body with lighter patches, most often with longitudinal lines. Found flattened on Turtle Grass.
Elongated emerald-green body with gray-pink patch on left of dorsum. Gray on rhinophores and oral tentacles.
A pelagic nudibranch species that is rarely seen drifting over a reef. Transparent body circled with white spots.
Robust shell with a large body whorl and vertical ribs. Sawtooth aperture lip. Feeds on bivalves (seen above).
Flattened oral disc with small warty projections in radiating lines. Numerous short pointed tentacles.
Pale purple gas-filled float with pinched edge along top. Purple trailing tentacles can reach up to 50 m / 160 ft.
Rounded fins, not connected at the rear. Short arms. Large chromatophores, commonly in red and orange.
Rounded fins, not connected at the rear. Often displays greenish-yellow. Found in seagrass and shallow reefs.
Distinctively hairy carapace and legs. Deep red claw arms with numerous short spines. Black tips on claws.
Extremely thin fan-shaped valves with rounded tops and faint radiating ribs. Translucent amber to gray in color.
Orange body with pointed orange and black cerata. Black rhinophores with pale blue or white posteriors.
Translucent body with green digestive tracts. Cerata on either side of body, leaving the white pericardium visible.
Elongated rounded body with short brown spines. Longer clear spines on the top and towards the rear.
Color varies from olive-green to reddish. Red or purple markings on legs and claws. Found on rocky inshores.
Bulbous bright yellow to orange sponge. Surface forms lumpy melted blobs around small excurrent openings.
Red to orange carapace with two pale lines towards the rear. Claw arms and legs bristled with short brown hairs.
Small individuals may show clusters of small white spots on dorsum and white bars or triangles on mantle edges.
Elongated claw arms, much wider than carapace, with distinctive sharp forward-facing teeth on the outer edge.
Rounded bumpy orange to red carapace with yellow markings lined in black, also on legs and claw arms.
Oval pink to reddish body with large eye near front of head. Pale fins. Distinctively rounded lobes on tail.
Rounded tubercles cover the dorsum. Highly variable in color: yellow, gold or purple. Short fused rhinophores.
Thick fan-shaped valves with large radial ribs. White with red or grayish lines. Found in shallow/intertidal areas.
Short, almost triangular rostrum. Commonly covered in filamentous red algae. Found in reef and rubble areas.
Globular body with two large siphons, often encrusted in algae. Incurrent siphon has thin inward-facing spines.
Brownish tunicates growing in clusters from a common tunic. Outer edges of the siphons are pale and mottled.
Three yellow bars. Blue spots on pelvic and anal fins. Squared tail with transparent margin (compare: P. paru).
Dark gray with yellow scale edges. Yellow around eye, at base of pectoral fin and on tips of dorsal and anal fins.
Radioles form a U-shaped crown. Red and yellow are the most common colors. Calcified tube usually hidden.
Reddish with white reticulated pattern on head, body and claw arms. Found inside living pen shells (p. 215).
Five broad arms tipped with black. Sometimes white markings on the top. Cryptic, found hiding in rubble.
Pale pink to reddish carapace and claws, all covered in bright white and purple spots, each circled in red.
A broad calcified crust of pink to gray alga that takes on the shape of the underlying reef, holding it in place.
Brown to gold with white blotches. Yellow edges on arms and claws. Found hiding in floats of sargassum (p. 24).
Uniformly red to silvery-pink body. May have darker red bar or spots beneath eye. Schools over deeper reefs.
Wide reddish to orange bars on head and body. Red bands on forehead. Red spots on tail and dorsal fin. Often upside down.
Coppery-orange body with broad black bars. Dark spots and bands on wide pectorals. Three black bars on tail
Juveniles have smaller pectorals and a distinctive dark spot at rear of first dorsal fin. Spot at center of tail base.
Brown body with three or four wide diagonal darker bars. Body and fins have reddish spots. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
Smooth elongated shell with a long thin spire. Dark brown to reddish. Mantle and proboscis are yellow.
Slender elongated body with dark spots and small bright yellow flap-like cirri. Found on Great Star Coral (p. 82).
Distinctively elongated snout. Orange bar above eye. Dorsal fins are darker, pelvic and anal fins are yellow.
Dark upper body and short snout. Gray dorsal and anal fins. Pectoral fin brownish with pale tip and lower edge.
White to reddish-brown body with distinctive thin dark lines and a white margin. Close-set pseudotentacles.
White body with a dense reticulated pattern of brown markings. Black lines on the head and around margin.
Translucent pear-shaped shell showing the animal’s markings underneath. Common in sandy shallow areas.
Orange teardrop-shaped shell with narrow white bands. Mantle with white blotches and dense orange spots.
Glossy cream to brownish shell with white spots and faint banding. Translucent mantle with large white spots.
White to yellowish shell with white spots and blotches and faint bands. Translucent mantle with white blotches.
Brownish body with darker blotches. Large white spots, denser along medial line. White-tipped pseudotentacles.
Dark purple body with orange spots outlined in black. Margin and pseudotentacles lined with small white spots.
Dark purple to black body with thin purple and orange lines around the margin. Orange-tipped pseudotentacles.
Body may be paler with tiny white speckles. Same thin white margin with irregular white lines pointing inwards.
Uniformly black body and pseudotentacles. Light gray underside. Found mostly in sandy areas. Circumtropical.
The dark area in the center of the body may be speckled with white spots. The mantle margin remains orange.
Reddish-brown body with large dark patch on gill cover. Dark tail, rear dorsal and anal fin. Shy, hides in crevices.
Narrow V-shaped notch on front of carapace. White- banded legs. Distinctive ridges on front of claw arms.
Pale brown body and dark, banded legs and claw arms. Distinctive blue on eyestalks, antennae and mouth-parts.
Tapering body. Long thin claws and a flattened tail. May have white bands. Usually found on Wire Coral (p. 91).
Dark gray with a long tapering forehead. Sickle-shaped dorsal fin and long narrow flippers with pointed tips.
Brown to light green body with white spots or stripes. Can change colors and pattern quickly as it moves.
White to pink body with three large reddish-black spots below dorsal fin. Blue lines from mouth to below eye.
Silvery blue body with golden saddles. Dark bars below eye. Black upper pectorals, usually held out to the sides.
Similar to Hovering Dartfish (below), but with a long pointed tail in adults and black or dark red margins on dorsal fin.
Bluish-gray to lavender with electric blue stripe on head. Rounded tail, not pointed. No black margin on dorsal fin.
Dark brown valves with a distinctive point near the hinge. Found attached to gorgonians, often heavily encrusted.
Bushy colony of long tapering branches. Each branch divided into X or Y shape when seen in cross section.
Short bushy colonies of flattened branches. Bright yellow with translucent white polyps on all branch edges.
Flattened purple branches in fan or bush shapes. White polyps extend from grooves running down both edges.
Red bars on head and body. Large pectoral arrays. Long dorsal and anal spines. Fleshy appendages around mouth.
Nearly transparent body with a long bulbous proboscis and dark eyes. Foot appears as a single swimming fin.
Pale grooved shell with dark patches. Brownish mantle covering the shell may have many knobby protrusions.
Elongated shell with a sharply pointed spire. Thin brown lines encircle the shell. White mantle. Buries into sand.
A colony of thousands of tunicate clones, joined to form a long cylindrical structure. Pelagic, rarely seen over reefs.
Pale brown body with reddish spots with black centers. Small black spots on face and head. Protruding nostrils.
Single white or pinkish polyp surrounded by numerous thin curling tentacles. Often found with amphipods (p. 265).
The most common sharksucker on Green Sea Turtles. May be seen aggressively feeding on the turtle’s waste.
Dark gray to black body covered in white spots and thin white lines. Three distinct ridges running along sides.
Short dense dark green leaves growing upwards from a central stalk. More elongated than similar species.
Flat olive-green leaves growing upwards from a central stalk. Leaves are not as dense and end in a blunt top.
Thin plate-like leaves growing upwards in a concentric pattern and forming a ball shape. Often pointed at the tip.
Dusky gray. Dorsal fin starts above pectoral edge. 2nd dorsal fin above middle of anal fin. Found in shallows.
Flattened disc with tentacles ending in forked projections. Shorter tentacles around rim. May be found in clusters.
Smooth rounded body with large incurrent and excurrent siphons. Color variable from brown to lavender or purple.
Grows singly or in dense clusters. This corallimorph is also able to fluoresce, seeming to give off its own light.
Brownish-gray with dense dark speckles. Often white bar at midbody. Low jaw on blunt snout. Found on sponges.
Light brown segments often with dark edges. Distinctive rounded W marking on tail. Usually attaches near gills.
Yellowish-brown with black spots. Spots on yellow-edged tail, dorsal and anal fins. Shy, often hides under ledges.
Grayish-brown body with white blotches and a pointed snout. Rounded fins and tail. Often found lying on its side.
Brown with dark spots behind eye and only few spots on body. Fins match body color. Found in shallow rubble.
The largest of the feather dusters, with a double row of banded radioles. Variable in color: tan, yellow or purple.
Encrusting tangles of thin white tubes that the tiny worms build as shelters. Tiny red radioles are visible at each tip.
Black body and pointed barbels, covered with small white spots. Found only in Belize and surrounding atolls.
Elongated brown body with broad head. Thick branching barbels around chin. Large blue eyes. Belize to Panama.
Black body with solid white lines on head and radiating from eyes. White spots on body. Found only in Belize.
Long thin fleshy barbels around chin, rarely branching. Large whitish nostrils in front of eyes. Yucatan to Belize.
Thin white lines on head. Yellow border on pectoral fins. Yellow ventral, dorsal and anal fins. Endemic to Yucatan.
Tough leathery greenish-brown leaves with darker brown blotches. Gas floats connect by a flattened stalk.
Sparse elongated brown leaves, often forking near the base. Attaches to the reef with a strong conical holdfast.
Long serrated blades with pale central vein. Gas floats are rounded. Gathers in wide mats of floating vegetation.
Elongated oval leaves with a whitish central vein. Olive to light brown. Often found growing in small clusters.
Narrow brown blades with serrated edges. No prominent central vein. Gas floats with distinctively pointed tips.
Thin leaves with toothed edges are sparsely distributed along a stalk attached to the reef. Fewer gas floats.
Slender light brown body with dark saddles. Large dark spots along the lateral line. Distinctive tiny oval pupil.
Olive-green with dark bars. Pearly spots on upper back and head. Distinctive mohawk of red and white filaments.
Dark blue body and fins. Paler blue facial markings. Large beak often covered in algae. Pointed lobes on tail.
Uniformly light blue body with gray area on cheek. All fins are blue. Distinctively blunt head. Small pale blue eyes.
The largest of the parrotfishes. Orange-bronze head and green body. Large scales. Orange tail with pointed lobes.
Pale body with three dark stripes from snout to tail base. Reddish eyes. Clear tail (compare: S. taeniopterus).
Pale body with three dark stripes from snout to tail base. Diffuse dark borders on tail (compare: S. iseri juvenile).
Brownish-gray upper body with diffuse white stripe from pectoral to lower tail base. No markings on a gray face.
Banded reddish-brown arms that only branch once or twice. Found on sea rods (p. 97). Curled up by day.
Small clumps of undulating masses formed by fine, hair-like filaments. Whitish with rose-red to purple highlights.
Pale, with brown stripes and small white spots. Yellowish tail. Often found among Masked/Glass Gobies (p. 295).
Creamy-brown dorsum with brownish spots. Two distinct rows of knobby tubercles run from rhinophores to gills.
Creamy-brown dorsum, often with darker patches. Large orange tubercles in a cauliflower shape, ringed in white.
Silvery with iridescent blue sheen. Distinctive lateral line drops sharply down below dorsal fin. Deeply forked tail.
Silvery to greenish-blue body. Rows of spots above and below a broken orange midbody stripe. Dark dorsal fin.
Pink to bright red on a pale background. Dark saddle above pectoral fin. Tail with red bar and red margin.
Tiny upside-down mushroom shapes over top of eye. Mottled red to brownish body. Two indistinct bars on tail.
The largest of the scorpionfishes. Pale tail with three dark bars. Often fleshy cirrus over eye. Color highly variable.
Dark brown to red body. Yellow spots on large pectoral fins and tail. Small black spot on rear of first dorsal fin.
Reddish-brown body. Black blotch at rear edge of first dorsal fin. White bar on tail base and red bar onto tail.
Light brown with white blotches or blue spots. Tall lobes on back. Found camouflaged in sargassum rafts (p. 24).
Rounded brown to orange body. Brown spots on legs. Smooth antennal plates. Distinctively purple antennae.
Silvery-blue body. Large eyes (larger than snout length). Mostly pelagic, but may form dense inshore schools.
Narrow silvery body with bluish tinge. Very steep head profile. Long dorsal and anal fins. Prefers shallow areas.
Delicate rounded shell with a low conical spire. Irregular reddish-brown bands. Light brown mantle.
The largest of the jacks. Oblique dark bar from snout, through eye to dorsal fin base. Pale dorsal and anal fins.
Silvery body with prominent dark fins. Oblique dark bar from snout, through eye to dorsal fin. Mostly pelagic.
Upper head and body dark orange. Two orange squares just behind eye, circled in black. Orange bar on snout.
Head and upper front of body mottled brown. Dark and orange bars on pale abdomen. Dark spots on tail base.
Brown head and upper body. Large white patch on front of abdomen. Blue stripes on face. Spots on tail borders.
Pale brown with dark brown spots and bars. Two dark ocelli at tail base. Large dark blotch on front of dorsal fin.
Reddish-brown. Black bars onto dorsal fin. Dark stripe through eye. Dark spots on tail. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
White body with broad orange stripe from chin to tail base. Dark saddles on upper body. Dark stripes on tail.
Pale tan with faint body bars. Dark stripe on snout. Row of irregular white blotches from behind eye to a clear tail.
Bluish-gray to pinkish-brown with darker bars across upper body. Pale blotches on side. Translucent fins.
Cream to reddish-brown disc. Red-banded arms with tiny spines. Found only on Rose Lace Coral (p. 73).
Unmarked mantle is uniform in color. Can be purple, pale pink or white, depending on the host gorgonian.
Small bright green shell with white spots or bands. Long thin tentacles. Found on Turtle Grass, on which it feeds.
Reddish stalks branch repeatedly, growing in a single plane like a fan. Covered with tiny white stinging polyps.
Rounded semi-transparent tubes of pale pink to red, branching near the tips and growing into tangled clumps.

Uniformly red body and tail. Pearly spots above pale abdomen. Red dorsal fin, yellow pelvic and anal fins. Greenish-brown upper body. Short blue bar at upper corner of gill cover. Dark spot at pectoral base. Red eye.
Solid greenish-blue body. All fins are pink to red, except a clear pectoral. Often a white spot at upper tail base.
Bluish-green body, pale abdomen. Dark spot at base of pectoral, large blue blotch behind it. Red crescent on tail.
Greenish-brown upper body and creamy white below. Often green blotch behind gill cover, onto pectoral base.
Uniformly greenish body. Dorsal, anal and pelvic fins tinged with red. Black spot at base of pectoral. Red eye.
Green body with pink stripes on cheek. Pink edge on gill cover, yellow spot at top corner. Yellow markings on tail.
Gray to light tan body with diffuse dark blotches behind pectoral fin. Two distinctive skin flaps on upper back.
Grayish-brown body. Distinctive row of black spots from chin to tail base. Pale abdomen. Two black bars on tail.
Pale cream body with dark patches and spots. White abdomen. Bright orange eyes. Shallow sandy areas.
Silvery-gray upper body with oblique bars. Pointed snout and protruding lower jaw. Dorsal and anal fins close to tail.
Elongated silvery body with faint yellow body stripes. Two dorsal fins are widely separated. Usually schooling.
Deep notches on very curved snout. Tall rounded dorsal fin. Straight rear edge on pelvic fin (compare: S. mokarran).
Slightly notched snout, only slightly curved. Tall pointed dorsal fin. Small eye. Curved rear edge on pelvic fin.
Pink encrusting sponge with red speckles. Excurrent openings are a lighter pink and slightly protruding.
Salmon to dull orange encrusting sponge with distinctively protruding star-shaped veins. Large excurrent openings.
Two spiraling crowns of radioles. Usually found in contrasting shades of purple, white and orange.
Only found on shallow corals or encrusting fire corals. Colors vary, often combinations of pastels and black.
Mucilaginous mass of tiny yellow filaments. Attaches to and eventually overgrows substrates and living corals.
Rounded deep red valves with numerous pointed spines on both top and bottom. Brownish mantle with white blotches.
Brick-red to pale orange. Upper valve with radial ribs and long flattened spines, lower valve cemented to the reef.
Golden brown fluffy tufts growing from a central axis. Found growing on rocky substrates on deeper reefs.
Pale orange with white spots on both head and dorsum. Lamellate rhinophores, shorter than the oral tentacles.
Translucent brown dorsum with white spots. Distinctive brown reticulated pattern, continuing onto rhinophores.
White spots on dorsum may join as patches, the sides are solid brown. Curved tapering cerata with white tips.
Dorsal and anal fins extend only to the end of the tail base. Thin dark border on lower edge of the anal fin.
Black to dark gray. Both dorsal and anal fins extend well beyond tail base. Bright blue leading edge on anal fin.
Blue spots on top of head form lines on snout. Thin blue edge on anal fin. Black spot outlined in blue on dorsal fin.
A common variation of S. partitus: white body with darker dorsal and anal fins, tail base and tail. Yellow pectoral fin.
Brown to tan. Dark spot at base of pectoral fin. Usually a dark spot at upper tail base. Yellow crescent above eye.
Dark body, often with yellow abdomen, anal fin and tail. Dark scale borders form lines. Often a dark spot at tail base.
Pointed rostrum has a distinctively white tip. Dark patch around eye. Larger or coastal individuals develop spots.
Elongated gray body with a long pointed rostrum. Dark streak near eye. Fast playful swimmers, often jumping.
Long narrow head with a sloping forehead and long rostrum. White patch above eye continues onto back.
Reddish tan with pointed lateral spines (often covered). Purplish claws. Often has Hitchhiking Anemone (p. 119).
Elongated body with narrow rounded plates. Plates are often mottled in white. Scaly gray to brown girdle.
Rear body and claws are white with wide red bands. Long white antennae. Pale blue eggs are often visible.
Golden to pale yellow body. Claws banded with white, red and orange. Red bands on tail. Long pale antennae.
Long tapering rostrum. Carapace with yellow and black stripes. Long legs with yellow stripes. Small blue claws.
Pale tan body with dark longitudinal lines of broken spots and dashes. Often found hiding in sargassum (p. 24).
The most common squid. Thin unbroken fin runs along the entire length of the body. Color is highly changeable.
Elongated, with rounded fins and long hunting tentacles. Photophore patch on dorsal mantle near head. Pelagic.
Flattened oral disc is completely covered with short rounded green or brown tentacles. Prefers shallows.
Spherical bell with a short skirt of tentacles below. Red, yellow or bluish markings, mostly around the rim.
Orange to brownish shell with pointed spines, always largest on final whorl. Aperture lip slants downwards.
Orange to brownish shell with evenly sized pointed spines on the whorls. Aperture lip slants upwards.
Distinctive reddish to orange highlights on dorsal and anal fins and lobes of tail. Long dark bar on gill cover.
Dark brown with short snout and overhanging mouth. Black vertical fins fused with tail. May act as a cleaner.
Tan body with thin brown lines and a large hump on the back. Long tapering tail. Tiny blue spots circled in red.
Pale brown to whitish body covered with scattered brown spots and blotches. Two long pointed nuchal tentacles.
Fan-shaped blades in shades of green to brown, with pale green bands. Grows in bushes from a central stalk.
Circular gray body with large gill openings behind eyes. Snout and tips of fins are rounded. Inshore shallows.
Eyes are distinctively close together. Two dark spots on lateral line: under laid-down pectoral and at base of tail.
Elongated yellowish body with four or five faint body bars. Dorsal and anal fins pale, darkening towards tail.
White to pale brownish body with scattered dots. Two distinctive black spots near tail base, circled in white.
Tiny individual tunicates growing from a common tunic that spreads across the substrate. Color varies greatly.
A very dense ball of fine hair-like filaments. Gray, blue-green or burgundy on the outer layer and paler inside.
Green to reddish-brown body with twelve feather-like feeding tentacles. Found in algae and seagrass beds.
Elongated snout with a dark stripe. Bright patch on gill cover. Thin dark bars along body. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
Brownish-gold body with thin pale bars and long snout. Found hiding in sargassum (p. 24). Rarely seen on reefs.
Gray to greenish-brown. Dark diamond-shaped blotches with pale centers. Row of pale spots along upper body.
Brown bars join diamond-shaped blotches on sides. Final bar is an inverted Y. Dark spot at upper edge of gill cover.
Brown to reddish body with small blue markings. Broken white line down side. Small dark spot just behind snout.
Tan to greenish body with long white lines or reticulated patterns. Rhinophores close-set, between parapodia.
Thin tubular green leaves growing in clumps that can cover a wide area. Often found among Turtle Grass.
Gelatinous bell with nematocyst warts. Four tentacles with red banding (may be retracted while swimming).
Rounded and flattened leaf with pale filamentous edges. Thin bright green or blue bands. Forms small clusters.
Small individuals are often darker brown, with radiating white spots on gill and rhinophore sheaths more visible.
Flattened gray to purple dorsum with dark blotches and raised central area. Brown rhinophores with white tips.
Rounded shell with a low spire. Spirals blotched in red and white over golden brown. Found in the shallows.
Flattened red carapace with white markings. Red and white bands on legs. Smooth red claws with white tips.
Cream to yellowish valves with broad radiating pink lines. Only empty valves are found; usually buried under sand.
Flattened disc, sometimes flecked with pale spots. Large bulbous tips on tapering tentacles. Variable in color.

Short grayish-blue bulbous tentacles, often with paler tips. Translucent pedal disc. Shallow reefs to intertidal. Pale tentacles become maroon towards the mouth. The oral disc has large pale blotches. Shallow reefs or sand. An undescribed species. Anemones in this genus are characterized by having swollen tips on the tentacles.
Thick-walled shells with pitted grooves. Opening covered by stony plates when not feeding. Shallow to intertidal.
Flat thin green leaves about 1.5 cm wide, with rounded tips. Covers vast areas, dominating other seagrasses.
Dark upper body with yellow stripe. White abdomen. Large black spot at front of dorsal fin. Other fins clear.
Flattened green bristed carapace and legs. Often holds strips of algae over head. Found on blade algae (p. 21).
Triangular carapace, similar to P. mutica (above), but covered in lumpy projections. Found among coral rubble.
Reddish body with large head and small tail. Pale spots with blue rims. Short antennae. Found on anemones.
Translucent body with highly variable green, brown and white highlights. Banded legs. Often near anemones.
Orange dorsum covered with distinctive sharply pointed dark papillae. Pale brown gills with six branching leaves.
Dark body with yellowish area on back of the head and rhinophores. Blue and orange half-moons on the sides.
Prominent curling rhinophores tipped in red. Parapodia lined with red, blue and green. Yellow Y on the head.

Branchlets alternate at right angles from a single stalk. Tiny polyps are found on both sides of the branchlets. Thick reddish-brown central stalk. Branchlets in a single plane with alternating polyps. Often found in clusters.
A long tubular mass of tiny purple eggs encased in a transparent jelly. The squid itself is from very deep water.
Translucent with two red patches on abdomen, outlined in black and then white. Similar dashes along spinal column.
Uniform dark brownish body with inconspicuous bars. Dark head with scattered small white spots. Dark fins.
Translucent head and body with prominent thin black bars. Found in coral rubble or sponges. From Florida.
Translucent with dark bars, more widely spaced towards rear. Pink head with brown bands. Dark spots below eye.
Pale head with red bands. Over 20 thin bars. Honduras/ Belize: T. multifasciatus elsewhere, without cheek bands.
Translucent with rows of oval brown spots. Two large brown patches on abdomen outlined only in white.
Dark brown bars edged with blue or blue spots. Second and third bars are an inverted Y. Thin dark bars from eye.
Pale brown body with six hourglass saddles down back. Dark bars under eye. Dark patch on upper pectoral base.
Plates are patterned in green, brown or red. Deep red girdle sometimes showing pale spots or thin bands.
Shell may be solid brown or with bands. Broad brown to black mantle with irregular white spots and long blotches.
Pale circular body with brown blotches, straight at front of head. Short snout. Rounded dorsal fins set close to tail.
Long slender body with a distinctive hump towards tail. Long tapering snout. Color varies, matching the host.
Elongated translucent body with red and white speckles and small hump. Found mostly on Sea Plumes (p. 101).
Pale blue and gold stripes down side. Black oval at top of gill cover. Distinctively blunt head and upturned mouth.
Tall silvery body, often with yellow blotch on abdomen and dusky oval patch behind dark pectoral. Dark anal fin.
Distinctively elongated black dorsal and anal fins. Four thin body bars. Often found schooling in the shallows.
Bulbous head with upturned rostrum and often a white line below eye. Abdominal sections have pointed edges.
Dark brown with white blotches and small orange spots. White lines run from rhinophores to large pale gills.
Encrusting calcified layer of larger pink to reddish zooids, visible to the naked eye. Often found at depth.
Long flattened segmented blades in pale to dark pink, often branching. Found growing from a common holdfast.
This overgrowing tunicate is most often a dull gray color but can be found in white, bluish-green or dark purple.
Rounded dark purple to black body. White spines are short and dense. Visible podia. Prefers shallow reefs.
Pink to translucent dorsum with a fine network of white to yellow reticulated lines. Short yellow branching cerata.
Smooth light brown body with thin white stripes. Short widely spaced tufts of cerata. Long tentacles on oral veil.
White speckles on a tapering gray to pinkish dorsum. Small branching cerata and a broad rounded oral veil.
Highly branched cerata surround the dorsum. Flattened oral veil with short tentacles. Sheathed rhinophores.
Flattened spiny carapace and white-banded legs. Found living within pits and galls on a variety of coral species.
Long dark red to black body. Often a pale stripe along the side. Found on Long Spine Sea Urchins (p. 149).
Triangular cone-shaped thickened leaves on a short stalk. Often with dark brown speckles and pale centers.
Tall erect stalks covered in triangular leaves with distinct rounded swellings in the centers. Found in the shallows.
Robust shell with a long tapering aperture lip. Mantle is mottled black and orange and is commonly buried.
Orange to brown shell with ridges and a long curving aperture lip. Pale mantle with dark reddish blotches.
Robust light to dark gray body with a paler abdomen. Short rostrum. Coastal individuals are usually smaller.
Bluish-green upper body, silvery below. Dark lateral keel at tail base. Lower tail lobe is longer than upper lobe.
Calcified green blade with a felt-like texture, as wide as it is tall. Narrow stalk. Often found in deeper sandy areas.
Thin ragged-edged cup of light green, attached by a thin stalk. Often found in sandy areas in large groupings.
Narrow fan shape extending out from a single stalk. Thick-walled. Dark green with pale edges on the blade.
Broad fan-shaped green blades on a single short stalk. Distinctive lines radiate to the edge of the blades.
Slender silvery body (may show faint bars). Concave below lower jaw. Large eye with dark spot above iris.
Pale central disc with a brown circle inside. Short brown tentacles. Found encrusting vase and rope sponges.
Rounded silvery to dusky body, sometimes with faint bars. Low triangular dorsal fin and tail, often darker gray.
Variable yellow to brown body with dark blotches and small yellow spots. Snout and tips of fins are rounded.
Transparent body with a rounded hump towards a long transparent tail. A second set of claws behind the first.
Clear bell, taller than it is wide. Four distinct orange radial canals. Up to 100 short bright orange tentacles.
Flat body with pale contractile tentacles from either end. Color matches substrate, with pink or brown highlights.
A spreading mat of pale to olive-green rounded or oval bubble shapes. Distinctive silvery sheen on the surface.
Clumps of dark green elongated bubbles, randomly branching with bulbous tips. Found in sheltered areas.
Large rounded dark to olive-green bubbles, often with a silvery sheen. May grow individually or in small clusters.
Dense clusters of shiny dark green filaments, growing to form a dense cushion. Found in calm shallow areas.
Thick-walled shell with a small pointed conical spire. Mantle is brown and speckled. Often covered in algae.
Elongated ribbed white shell with thin dark bands above each whorl. Often a broader dark band on the final whorl.
Highly ridged dark red shell with yellow and black bands. Black mantle and proboscis. White-banded tentacles.
Bright orange shell with a sharply pointed spire. Bands of small black markings. Single white band on final whorl.
Rounded shell with bulbous spire. Wavy zigzag lines and spots. Dominant color may be black, green or reddish.
Pale brown thick-walled shell circled with distinctive thin brown lines. White mantle with brown and gold markings.
Pale oblong shell with three broad brown bands (may be diffuse). Orange spots on white mantle and proboscis.
Pale oblong shell with dark mottling. Knobby texture on white-spotted mantle. Dark banding on pale tentacles.
Elongated orange shell with pale stripes. Mantle and proboscis solid orange or pale with dark orange spots.
Orange-yellow with a thin yellow stripe. Thin blue line on head. Rare, found among deep Masked/Glass Gobies.
Flattened gray to pinkish carapace with highly variable markings. Numerous blunt spines protruding from sides.
Tall bushy pink alga with long thin central branches. Smaller light pink branchlets grow out in a single plane.
Tangled masses of fine hair-like deep red filaments, each repeatedly branching outwards. May cover large areas.
Purple body with rows of small black spots. Three dark lines on cheek. White patch above eye. Tail edged in red.
Flat-bottomed conical shell, typically with other shells and pieces of coral debris cemented on for protection.
Yellow cheek and gill cover with thin blue bars. Vertical blue lines on scales. Darkened pectoral base. Red iris.
The largest of the razorfishes. Blue lines under a small eye. Blunt forehead. Often a single diffuse body bar.
Pink to reddish with dark edges on scales. Pale blue bars on lower head. Often a blue spot on dorsal fin. Red iris.
Bushy, with slightly flattened red branches. Branchlets are short with darker tips. Prefers areas of strong surge.
Small elongated bell with a pointed tip. Four long trailing tentacles with distinctive rows of small white bulbs.
Color and density are highly variable, in loose patches of individuals or in clusters. May show bright fluorescence.
